1.play: Enter GLOUCESTER, solus

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Now is the winter of our discontent

3.play: Made glorious summer by this sun of York;

4.play: And all the clouds that lour'd upon our house

5.play: In the deep bosom of the ocean buried.

6.play: Now are our brows bound with victorious wreaths;

7.play: Our bruised arms hung up for monuments;

8.play: Our stern alarums changed to merry meetings,

9.play: Our dreadful marches to delightful measures.

10.play: Grim-visaged war hath smooth'd his wrinkled front;

11.play: And now, instead of mounting barbed steeds

12.play: To fright the souls of fearful adversaries,

13.play: He capers nimbly in a lady's chamber

14.play: To the lascivious pleasing of a lute.

15.play: But I, that am not shaped for sportive tricks,

16.play: Nor made to court an amorous looking-glass;

17.play: I, that am rudely stamp'd, and want love's majesty

18.play: To strut before a wanton ambling nymph;

19.play: I, that am curtail'd of this fair proportion,

20.play: Cheated of feature by dissembling nature,

21.play: Deformed, unfinish'd, sent before my time

22.play: Into this breathing world, scarce half made up,

23.play: And that so lamely and unfashionable

24.play: That dogs bark at me as I halt by them;

25.play: Why, I, in this weak piping time of peace,

26.play: Have no delight to pass away the time,

27.play: Unless to spy my shadow in the sun

28.play: And descant on mine own deformity:

29.play: And therefore, since I cannot prove a lover,

30.play: To entertain these fair well-spoken days,

31.play: I am determined to prove a villain

32.play: And hate the idle pleasures of these days.

33.play: Plots have I laid, inductions dangerous,

34.play: By drunken prophecies, libels and dreams,

35.play: To set my brother Clarence and the king

36.play: In deadly hate the one against the other:

37.play: And if King Edward be as true and just

38.play: As I am subtle, false and treacherous,

39.play: This day should Clarence closely be mew'd up,

40.play: About a prophecy, which says that 'G'

41.play: Of Edward's heirs the murderer shall be.

42.play: Dive, thoughts, down to my soul: here

44.play: Brother, good day; what means this armed guard

47.play: Tendering my person's safety, hath appointed

48.play: This conduct to convey me to the Tower.

50.play: CLARENCE: Because my name is George.

51.play: GLOUCESTER: Alack, my lord, that fault is none of yours;

52.play: He should, for that, commit your godfathers:

53.play: O, belike his majesty hath some intent

54.play: That you shall be new-christen'd in the Tower.

55.play: But what's the matter, Clarence?  may I know?

56.play: CLARENCE: Yea, Richard, when I know; for I protest

57.play: As yet I do not: but, as I can learn,

58.play: He hearkens after prophecies and dreams;

59.play: And from the cross-row plucks the letter G.

60.play: And says a wizard told him that by G

62.play: And, for my name of George begins with G,

63.play: It follows in his thought that I am he.

64.play: These, as I learn, and such like toys as these

65.play: Have moved his highness to commit me now.

66.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, this it is, when men are ruled by women:

67.play: 'Tis not the king that sends you to the Tower:

68.play: My Lady Grey his wife, Clarence, 'tis she

69.play: That tempers him to this extremity.

70.play: Was it not she and that good man of worship,

72.play: That made him send Lord Hastings to the Tower,

73.play: From whence this present day he is deliver'd?

74.play: We are not safe, Clarence; we are not safe.

75.play: CLARENCE: By heaven, I think there's no man is secure

76.play: But the queen's kindred and night-walking heralds

77.play: That trudge betwixt the king and Mistress Shore.

78.play: Heard ye not what an humble suppliant

79.play: Lord hastings was to her for his delivery?

80.play: GLOUCESTER: Humbly complaining to her deity

81.play: Got my lord chamberlain his liberty.

82.play: I'll tell you what; I think it is our way,

83.play: If we will keep in favour with the king,

84.play: To be her men and wear her livery:

85.play: The jealous o'erworn widow and herself,

86.play: Since that our brother dubb'd them gentlewomen.

87.play: Are mighty gossips in this monarchy.

88.play: BRAKENBURY: I beseech your graces both to pardon me;

89.play: His majesty hath straitly given in charge

90.play: That no man shall have private conference,

91.play: Of what degree soever, with his brother.

92.play: GLOUCESTER: Even so; an't please your worship, Brakenbury,

93.play: You may partake of any thing we say:

94.play: We speak no treason, man: we say the king

95.play: Is wise and virtuous, and his noble queen

96.play: Well struck in years, fair, and not jealous;

97.play: We say that Shore's wife hath a pretty foot,

98.play: A cherry lip, a bonny eye, a passing pleasing tongue;

99.play: And that the queen's kindred are made gentle-folks:

100.play: How say you sir? Can you deny all this?

101.play: BRAKENBURY: With this, my lord, myself have nought to do.

102.play: GLOUCESTER: Naught to do with mistress Shore! I tell thee, fellow,

103.play: He that doth naught with her, excepting one,

104.play: Were best he do it secretly, alone.

106.play: GLOUCESTER: Her husband, knave: wouldst thou betray me?

107.play: BRAKENBURY: I beseech your grace to pardon me, and withal

108.play: Forbear your conference with the noble duke.

109.play: CLARENCE: We know thy charge, Brakenbury, and will obey.

110.play: GLOUCESTER: We are the queen's abjects, and must obey.

111.play: Brother, farewell: I will unto the king;

112.play: And whatsoever you will employ me in,

113.play: Were it to call King Edward's widow sister,

114.play: I will perform it to enfranchise you.

115.play: Meantime, this deep disgrace in brotherhood

116.play: Touches me deeper than you can imagine.

117.play: CLARENCE: I know it pleaseth neither of us well.

118.play: GLOUCESTER: Well, your imprisonment shall not be long;

121.play: GLOUCESTER: Go, tread the path that thou shalt ne'er return.

122.play: Simple, plain Clarence! I do love thee so,

123.play: That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven,

124.play: If heaven will take the present at our hands.

125.play: But who comes here? the new-deliver'd Hastings?

126.play: HASTINGS: Good time of day unto my gracious lord!

127.play: GLOUCESTER: As much unto my good lord chamberlain!

128.play: Well are you welcome to the open air.

129.play: How hath your lordship brook'd imprisonment?

130.play: HASTINGS: With patience, noble lord, as prisoners must:

131.play: But I shall live, my lord, to give them thanks

132.play: That were the cause of my imprisonment.

133.play: GLOUCESTER: No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too;

134.play: For they that were your enemies are his,

135.play: And have prevail'd as much on him as you.

136.play: HASTINGS: More pity that the eagle should be mew'd,

137.play: While kites and buzzards prey at liberty.

139.play: HASTINGS: No news so bad abroad as this at home;

140.play: The King is sickly, weak and melancholy,

141.play: And his physicians fear him mightily.

142.play: GLOUCESTER: Now, by Saint Paul, this news is bad indeed.

143.play: O, he hath kept an evil diet long,

144.play: And overmuch consumed his royal person:

145.play: 'Tis very grievous to be thought upon.

146.play: What, is he in his bed?

148.play: GLOUCESTER: Go you before, and I will follow you.

149.play: He cannot live, I hope; and must not die

150.play: Till George be pack'd with post-horse up to heaven.

151.play: I'll in, to urge his hatred more to Clarence,

152.play: With lies well steel'd with weighty arguments;

153.play: And, if I fall not in my deep intent,

154.play: Clarence hath not another day to live:

155.play: Which done, God take King Edward to his mercy,

156.play: And leave the world for me to bustle in!

157.play: For then I'll marry Warwick's youngest daughter.

158.play: What though I kill'd her husband and her father?

159.play: The readiest way to make the wench amends

160.play: Is to become her husband and her father:

161.play: The which will I; not all so much for love

162.play: As for another secret close intent,

163.play: By marrying her which I must reach unto.

164.play: But yet I run before my horse to market:

165.play: Clarence still breathes; Edward still lives and reigns:

166.play: When they are gone, then must I count my gains.

1.play: Enter the corpse of KING HENRY the Sixth, Gentlemen with halberds to guard it; LADY ANNE being the mourner

2.play: LADY ANNE: Set down, set down your honourable load,

3.play: If honour may be shrouded in a hearse,

4.play: Whilst I awhile obsequiously lament

5.play: The untimely fall of virtuous Lancaster.

6.play: Poor key-cold figure of a holy king!

7.play: Pale ashes of the house of Lancaster!

8.play: Thou bloodless remnant of that royal blood!

9.play: Be it lawful that I invocate thy ghost,

10.play: To hear the lamentations of Poor Anne,

11.play: Wife to thy Edward, to thy slaughter'd son,

12.play: Stabb'd by the selfsame hand that made these wounds!

13.play: Lo, in these windows that let forth thy life,

14.play: I pour the helpless balm of my poor eyes.

15.play: Cursed be the hand that made these fatal holes!

16.play: Cursed be the heart that had the heart to do it!

17.play: Cursed the blood that let this blood from hence!

18.play: More direful hap betide that hated wretch,

19.play: That makes us wretched by the death of thee,

20.play: Than I can wish to adders, spiders, toads,

21.play: Or any creeping venom'd thing that lives!

22.play: If ever he have child, abortive be it,

23.play: Prodigious, and untimely brought to light,

24.play: Whose ugly and unnatural aspect

25.play: May fright the hopeful mother at the view;

26.play: And that be heir to his unhappiness!

27.play: If ever he have wife, let her he made

28.play: A miserable by the death of him

29.play: As I am made by my poor lord and thee!

30.play: Come, now towards Chertsey with your holy load,

31.play: Taken from Paul's to be interred there;

32.play: And still, as you are weary of the weight,

33.play: Rest you, whiles I lament King Henry's corse.

34.play: GLOUCESTER: Stay, you that bear the corse, and set it down.

35.play: LADY ANNE: What black magician conjures up this fiend,

37.play: GLOUCESTER: Villains, set down the corse; or, by Saint Paul,

38.play: I'll make a corse of him that disobeys.

39.play: Unmanner'd dog! stand thou, when I command:

40.play: Advance thy halbert higher than my breast,

41.play: Or, by Saint Paul, I'll strike thee to my foot,

42.play: And spurn upon thee, beggar, for thy boldness.

43.play: LADY ANNE: What, do you tremble? are you all afraid?

44.play: Alas, I blame you not; for you are mortal,

45.play: And mortal eyes cannot endure the devil.

46.play: Avaunt, thou dreadful minister of hell!

47.play: Thou hadst but power over his mortal body,

48.play: His soul thou canst not have; therefore be gone.

49.play: GLOUCESTER: Sweet saint, for charity, be not so curst.

50.play: LADY ANNE: Foul devil, for God's sake, hence, and trouble us not;

51.play: For thou hast made the happy earth thy hell,

52.play: Fill'd it with cursing cries and deep exclaims.

53.play: If thou delight to view thy heinous deeds,

54.play: Behold this pattern of thy butcheries.

55.play: O, gentlemen, see, see! dead Henry's wounds

56.play: Open their congeal'd mouths and bleed afresh!

57.play: Blush, Blush, thou lump of foul deformity;

58.play: For 'tis thy presence that exhales this blood

59.play: From cold and empty veins, where no blood dwells;

62.play: O God, which this blood madest, revenge his death!

63.play: O earth, which this blood drink'st revenge his death!

64.play: Either heaven with lightning strike the

66.play: Or earth, gape open wide and eat him quick,

67.play: As thou dost swallow up this good king's blood

68.play: Which his hell-govern'd arm hath butchered!

69.play: GLOUCESTER: Lady, you know no rules of charity,

70.play: Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.

71.play: LADY ANNE: Villain, thou know'st no law of God nor man:

72.play: No beast so fierce but knows some touch of pity.

73.play: GLOUCESTER: But I know none, and therefore am no beast.

74.play: LADY ANNE: O wonderful, when devils tell the truth!

75.play: GLOUCESTER: More wonderful, when angels are so angry.

76.play: Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman,

77.play: Of these supposed-evils, to give me leave,

78.play: By circumstance, but to acquit myself.

79.play: LADY ANNE: Vouchsafe, defused infection of a man,

80.play: For these known evils, but to give me leave,

81.play: By circumstance, to curse thy cursed self.

82.play: GLOUCESTER: Fairer than tongue can name thee, let me have

83.play: Some patient leisure to excuse myself.

84.play: LADY ANNE: Fouler than heart can think thee, thou canst make

85.play: No excuse current, but to hang thyself.

86.play: GLOUCESTER: By such despair, I should accuse myself.

87.play: LADY ANNE: And, by despairing, shouldst thou stand excused;

88.play: For doing worthy vengeance on thyself,

89.play: Which didst unworthy slaughter upon others.

90.play: GLOUCESTER: Say that I slew them not?

91.play: LADY ANNE: Why, then they are not dead:

92.play: But dead they are, and devilish slave, by thee.

93.play: GLOUCESTER: I did not kill your husband.

94.play: LADY ANNE: Why, then he is alive.

95.play: GLOUCESTER: Nay, he is dead; and slain by Edward's hand.

96.play: LADY ANNE: In thy foul throat thou liest: Queen Margaret saw

97.play: Thy murderous falchion smoking in his blood;

98.play: The which thou once didst bend against her breast,

99.play: But that thy brothers beat aside the point.

100.play: GLOUCESTER: I was provoked by her slanderous tongue,

101.play: which laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.

102.play: LADY ANNE: Thou wast provoked by thy bloody mind.

103.play: Which never dreamt on aught but butcheries:

104.play: Didst thou not kill this king?

106.play: LADY ANNE: Dost grant me, hedgehog? then, God grant me too

107.play: Thou mayst be damned for that wicked deed!

108.play: O, he was gentle, mild, and virtuous!

109.play: GLOUCESTER: The fitter for the King of heaven, that hath him.

110.play: LADY ANNE: He is in heaven, where thou shalt never come.

111.play: GLOUCESTER: Let him thank me, that holp to send him thither;

112.play: For he was fitter for that place than earth.

113.play: LADY ANNE: And thou unfit for any place but hell.

114.play: GLOUCESTER: Yes, one place else, if you will hear me name it.

117.play: LADY ANNE: Ill rest betide the chamber where thou liest!

118.play: GLOUCESTER: So will it, madam till I lie with you.

120.play: GLOUCESTER: I know so. But, gentle Lady Anne,

121.play: To leave this keen encounter of our wits,

122.play: And fall somewhat into a slower method,

123.play: Is not the causer of the timeless deaths

124.play: Of these Plantagenets, Henry and Edward,

126.play: LADY ANNE: Thou art the cause, and most accursed effect.

127.play: GLOUCESTER: Your beauty was the cause of that effect;

128.play: Your beauty: which did haunt me in my sleep

129.play: To undertake the death of all the world,

130.play: So I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.

131.play: LADY ANNE: If I thought that, I tell thee, homicide,

132.play: These nails should rend that beauty from my cheeks.

133.play: GLOUCESTER: These eyes could never endure sweet beauty's wreck;

134.play: You should not blemish it, if I stood by:

135.play: As all the world is cheered by the sun,

136.play: So I by that; it is my day, my life.

137.play: LADY ANNE: Black night o'ershade thy day, and death thy life!

138.play: GLOUCESTER: Curse not thyself, fair creature thou art both.

139.play: LADY ANNE: I would I were, to be revenged on thee.

140.play: GLOUCESTER: It is a quarrel most unnatural,

141.play: To be revenged on him that loveth you.

142.play: LADY ANNE: It is a quarrel just and reasonable,

143.play: To be revenged on him that slew my husband.

144.play: GLOUCESTER: He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband,

145.play: Did it to help thee to a better husband.

146.play: LADY ANNE: His better doth not breathe upon the earth.

147.play: GLOUCESTER: He lives that loves thee better than he could.

150.play: LADY ANNE: Why, that was he.

151.play: GLOUCESTER: The selfsame name, but one of better nature.

154.play: Why dost thou spit at me?

155.play: LADY ANNE: Would it were mortal poison, for thy sake!

156.play: GLOUCESTER: Never came poison from so sweet a place.

157.play: LADY ANNE: Never hung poison on a fouler toad.

158.play: Out of my sight! thou dost infect my eyes.

159.play: GLOUCESTER: Thine eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.

160.play: LADY ANNE: Would they were basilisks, to strike thee dead!

161.play: GLOUCESTER: I would they were, that I might die at once;

162.play: For now they kill me with a living death.

163.play: Those eyes of thine from mine have drawn salt tears,

164.play: Shamed their aspect with store of childish drops:

165.play: These eyes that never shed remorseful tear,

166.play: No, when my father York and Edward wept,

167.play: To hear the piteous moan that Rutland made

168.play: When black-faced Clifford shook his sword at him;

169.play: Nor when thy warlike father, like a child,

170.play: Told the sad story of my father's death,

171.play: And twenty times made pause to sob and weep,

172.play: That all the standers-by had wet their cheeks

173.play: Like trees bedash'd with rain: in that sad time

174.play: My manly eyes did scorn an humble tear;

175.play: And what these sorrows could not thence exhale,

176.play: Thy beauty hath, and made them blind with weeping.

177.play: I never sued to friend nor enemy;

178.play: My tongue could never learn sweet smoothing word;

179.play: But now thy beauty is proposed my fee,

180.play: My proud heart sues, and prompts my tongue to speak.

181.play: Teach not thy lips such scorn, for they were made

182.play: For kissing, lady, not for such contempt.

183.play: If thy revengeful heart cannot forgive,

184.play: Lo, here I lend thee this sharp-pointed sword;

185.play: Which if thou please to hide in this true bosom.

186.play: And let the soul forth that adoreth thee,

187.play: I lay it naked to the deadly stroke,

188.play: And humbly beg the death upon my knee.

189.play: Nay, do not pause; for I did kill King Henry,

190.play: But 'twas thy beauty that provoked me.

191.play: Nay, now dispatch; 'twas I that stabb'd young Edward,

192.play: But 'twas thy heavenly face that set me on.

193.play: Take up the sword again, or take up me.

194.play: LADY ANNE: Arise, dissembler: though I wish thy death,

195.play: I will not be the executioner.

196.play: GLOUCESTER: Then bid me kill myself, and I will do it.

198.play: GLOUCESTER: Tush, that was in thy rage:

199.play: Speak it again, and, even with the word,

200.play: That hand, which, for thy love, did kill thy love,

201.play: Shall, for thy love, kill a far truer love;

202.play: To both their deaths thou shalt be accessary.

203.play: LADY ANNE: I would I knew thy heart.

205.play: LADY ANNE: I fear me both are false.

206.play: GLOUCESTER: Then never man was true.

207.play: LADY ANNE: Well, well, put up your sword.

208.play: GLOUCESTER: Say, then, my peace is made.

209.play: LADY ANNE: That shall you know hereafter.

210.play: GLOUCESTER: But shall I live in hope?

211.play: LADY ANNE: All men, I hope, live so.

212.play: GLOUCESTER: Vouchsafe to wear this ring.

213.play: LADY ANNE: To take is not to give.

214.play: GLOUCESTER: Look, how this ring encompasseth finger.

215.play: Even so thy breast encloseth my poor heart;

216.play: Wear both of them, for both of them are thine.

217.play: And if thy poor devoted suppliant may

218.play: But beg one favour at thy gracious hand,

219.play: Thou dost confirm his happiness for ever.

221.play: GLOUCESTER: That it would please thee leave these sad designs

222.play: To him that hath more cause to be a mourner,

223.play: And presently repair to Crosby Place;

224.play: Where, after I have solemnly interr'd

225.play: At Chertsey monastery this noble king,

226.play: And wet his grave with my repentant tears,

227.play: I will with all expedient duty see you:

228.play: For divers unknown reasons. I beseech you,

230.play: LADY ANNE: With all my heart; and much it joys me too,

231.play: To see you are become so penitent.

232.play: Tressel and Berkeley, go along with me.

234.play: LADY ANNE: 'Tis more than you deserve;

235.play: But since you teach me how to flatter you,

236.play: Imagine I have said farewell already.

237.play: GLOUCESTER: Sirs, take up the corse.

239.play: GLOUCESTER: No, to White-Friars; there attend my coining.

240.play: Was ever woman in this humour woo'd?

241.play: Was ever woman in this humour won?

242.play: I'll have her; but I will not keep her long.

243.play: What! I, that kill'd her husband and his father,

244.play: To take her in her heart's extremest hate,

245.play: With curses in her mouth, tears in her eyes,

246.play: The bleeding witness of her hatred by;

247.play: Having God, her conscience, and these bars

249.play: And I nothing to back my suit at all,

250.play: But the plain devil and dissembling looks,

251.play: And yet to win her, all the world to nothing!

253.play: Hath she forgot already that brave prince,

254.play: Edward, her lord, whom I, some three months since,

255.play: Stabb'd in my angry mood at Tewksbury?

256.play: A sweeter and a lovelier gentleman,

257.play: Framed in the prodigality of nature,

258.play: Young, valiant, wise, and, no doubt, right royal,

259.play: The spacious world cannot again afford

260.play: And will she yet debase her eyes on me,

261.play: That cropp'd the golden prime of this sweet prince,

262.play: And made her widow to a woful bed?

263.play: On me, whose all not equals Edward's moiety?

264.play: On me, that halt and am unshapen thus?

265.play: My dukedom to a beggarly denier,

266.play: I do mistake my person all this while:

267.play: Upon my life, she finds, although I cannot,

268.play: Myself to be a marvellous proper man.

269.play: I'll be at charges for a looking-glass,

270.play: And entertain some score or two of tailors,

271.play: To study fashions to adorn my body:

272.play: Since I am crept in favour with myself,

273.play: Will maintain it with some little cost.

274.play: But first I'll turn yon fellow in his grave;

275.play: And then return lamenting to my love.

276.play: Shine out, fair sun, till I have bought a glass,

277.play: That I may see my shadow as I pass.

1.play: Enter QUEEN ELIZABETH, RIVERS, and GREY

2.play: RIVERS: Have patience, madam: there's no doubt his majesty

3.play: Will soon recover his accustom'd health.

4.play: GREY: In that you brook it in, it makes him worse:

5.play: Therefore, for God's sake, entertain good comfort,

6.play: And cheer his grace with quick and merry words.

7.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: If he were dead, what would betide of me?

8.play: RIVERS: No other harm but loss of such a lord.

9.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: The loss of such a lord includes all harm.

10.play: GREY: The heavens have bless'd you with a goodly son,

11.play: To be your comforter when he is gone.

12.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Oh, he is young and his minority

13.play: Is put unto the trust of Richard Gloucester,

14.play: A man that loves not me, nor none of you.

15.play: RIVERS: Is it concluded that he shall be protector?

16.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: It is determined, not concluded yet:

17.play: But so it must be, if the king miscarry.

18.play: GREY: Here come the lords of Buckingham and Derby.

19.play: BUCKINGHAM: Good time of day unto your royal grace!

20.play: DERBY: God make your majesty joyful as you have been!

21.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: The Countess Richmond, good my Lord of Derby.

22.play: To your good prayers will scarcely say amen.

23.play: Yet, Derby, notwithstanding she's your wife,

24.play: And loves not me, be you, good lord, assured

25.play: I hate not you for her proud arrogance.

26.play: DERBY: I do beseech you, either not believe

27.play: The envious slanders of her false accusers;

28.play: Or, if she be accused in true report,

29.play: Bear with her weakness, which, I think proceeds

30.play: From wayward sickness, and no grounded malice.

31.play: RIVERS: Saw you the king to-day, my Lord of Derby?

32.play: DERBY: But now the Duke of Buckingham and I

33.play: Are come from visiting his majesty.

34.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: What likelihood of his amendment, lords?

35.play: BUCKINGHAM: Madam, good hope; his grace speaks cheerfully.

36.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: God grant him health! Did you confer with him?

37.play: BUCKINGHAM: Madam, we did: he desires to make atonement

38.play: Betwixt the Duke of Gloucester and your brothers,

39.play: And betwixt them and my lord chamberlain;

40.play: And sent to warn them to his royal presence.

41.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Would all were well! but that will never be

42.play: I fear our happiness is at the highest.

43.play: GLOUCESTER: They do me wrong, and I will not endure it:

44.play: Who are they that complain unto the king,

45.play: That I, forsooth, am stern, and love them not?

46.play: By holy Paul, they love his grace but lightly

47.play: That fill his ears with such dissentious rumours.

48.play: Because I cannot flatter and speak fair,

49.play: Smile in men's faces, smooth, deceive and cog,

50.play: Duck with French nods and apish courtesy,

51.play: I must be held a rancorous enemy.

52.play: Cannot a plain man live and think no harm,

53.play: But thus his simple truth must be abused

55.play: RIVERS: To whom in all this presence speaks your grace?

56.play: GLOUCESTER: To thee, that hast nor honesty nor grace.

57.play: When have I injured thee? when done thee wrong?

58.play: Or thee? or thee? or any of your faction?

59.play: A plague upon you all! His royal person,--

60.play: Whom God preserve better than you would wish!--

61.play: Cannot be quiet scarce a breathing-while,

62.play: But you must trouble him with lewd complaints.

63.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Brother of Gloucester, you mistake the matter.

64.play: The king, of his own royal disposition,

65.play: And not provoked by any suitor else;

66.play: Aiming, belike, at your interior hatred,

67.play: Which in your outward actions shows itself

68.play: Against my kindred, brothers, and myself,

69.play: Makes him to send; that thereby he may gather

70.play: The ground of your ill-will, and so remove it.

71.play: GLOUCESTER: I cannot tell: the world is grown so bad,

72.play: That wrens make prey where eagles dare not perch:

73.play: Since every Jack became a gentleman

74.play: There's many a gentle person made a Jack.

75.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Come, come, we know your meaning, brother

77.play: You envy my advancement and my friends':

78.play: God grant we never may have need of you!

79.play: GLOUCESTER: Meantime, God grants that we have need of you:

80.play: Your brother is imprison'd by your means,

82.play: Held in contempt; whilst many fair promotions

83.play: Are daily given to ennoble those

84.play: That scarce, some two days since, were worth a noble.

85.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: By Him that raised me to this careful height

86.play: From that contented hap which I enjoy'd,

87.play: I never did incense his majesty

88.play: Against the Duke of Clarence, but have been

89.play: An earnest advocate to plead for him.

90.play: My lord, you do me shameful injury,

91.play: Falsely to draw me in these vile suspects.

92.play: GLOUCESTER: You may deny that you were not the cause

93.play: Of my Lord Hastings' late imprisonment.

94.play: RIVERS: She may, my lord, for--

95.play: GLOUCESTER: She may, Lord Rivers! why, who knows not so?

96.play: She may do more, sir, than denying that:

97.play: She may help you to many fair preferments,

98.play: And then deny her aiding hand therein,

99.play: And lay those honours on your high deserts.

100.play: What may she not? She may, yea, marry, may she--

102.play: GLOUCESTER: What, marry, may she! marry with a king,

103.play: A bachelor, a handsome stripling too:

104.play: I wis your grandam had a worser match.

105.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: My Lord of Gloucester, I have too long borne

106.play: Your blunt upbraidings and your bitter scoffs:

107.play: By heaven, I will acquaint his majesty

108.play: With those gross taunts I often have endured.

109.play: I had rather be a country servant-maid

110.play: Than a great queen, with this condition,

111.play: To be thus taunted, scorn'd, and baited at:

112.play: Small joy have I in being England's queen.

113.play: QUEEN MARGARET: And lessen'd be that small, God, I beseech thee!

114.play: Thy honour, state and seat is due to me.

115.play: GLOUCESTER: What! threat you me with telling of the king?

116.play: Tell him, and spare not: look, what I have said

117.play: I will avouch in presence of the king:

118.play: I dare adventure to be sent to the Tower.

119.play: 'Tis time to speak; my pains are quite forgot.

120.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Out, devil! I remember them too well:

121.play: Thou slewest my husband Henry in the Tower,

122.play: And Edward, my poor son, at Tewksbury.

123.play: GLOUCESTER: Ere you were queen, yea, or your husband king,

124.play: I was a pack-horse in his great affairs;

125.play: A weeder-out of his proud adversaries,

126.play: A liberal rewarder of his friends:

127.play: To royalize his blood I spilt mine own.

128.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Yea, and much better blood than his or thine.

129.play: GLOUCESTER: In all which time you and your husband Grey

130.play: Were factious for the house of Lancaster;

131.play: And, Rivers, so were you. Was not your husband

132.play: In Margaret's battle at Saint Alban's slain?

133.play: Let me put in your minds, if you forget,

134.play: What you have been ere now, and what you are;

135.play: Withal, what I have been, and what I am.

136.play: QUEEN MARGARET: A murderous villain, and so still thou art.

137.play: GLOUCESTER: Poor Clarence did forsake his father, Warwick;

138.play: Yea, and forswore himself,--which Jesu pardon!--

140.play: GLOUCESTER: To fight on Edward's party for the crown;

141.play: And for his meed, poor lord, he is mew'd up.

142.play: I would to God my heart were flint, like Edward's;

143.play: Or Edward's soft and pitiful, like mine

144.play: I am too childish-foolish for this world.

145.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Hie thee to hell for shame, and leave the world,

146.play: Thou cacodemon! there thy kingdom is.

147.play: RIVERS: My Lord of Gloucester, in those busy days

148.play: Which here you urge to prove us enemies,

149.play: We follow'd then our lord, our lawful king:

150.play: So should we you, if you should be our king.

151.play: GLOUCESTER: If I should be! I had rather be a pedlar:

152.play: Far be it from my heart, the thought of it!

153.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: As little joy, my lord, as you suppose

154.play: You should enjoy, were you this country's king,

155.play: As little joy may you suppose in me.

156.play: That I enjoy, being the queen thereof.

157.play: QUEEN MARGARET: A little joy enjoys the queen thereof;

158.play: For I am she, and altogether joyless.

159.play: I can no longer hold me patient.

160.play: Hear me, you wrangling pirates, that fall out

161.play: In sharing that which you have pill'd from me!

162.play: Which of you trembles not that looks on me?

163.play: If not, that, I being queen, you bow like subjects,

164.play: Yet that, by you deposed, you quake like rebels?

165.play: O gentle villain, do not turn away!

166.play: GLOUCESTER: Foul wrinkled witch, what makest thou in my sight?

167.play: QUEEN MARGARET: But repetition of what thou hast marr'd;

168.play: That will I make before I let thee go.

169.play: GLOUCESTER: Wert thou not banished on pain of death?

170.play: QUEEN MARGARET: I was; but I do find more pain in banishment

171.play: Than death can yield me here by my abode.

172.play: A husband and a son thou owest to me;

173.play: And thou a kingdom; all of you allegiance:

174.play: The sorrow that I have, by right is yours,

175.play: And all the pleasures you usurp are mine.

176.play: GLOUCESTER: The curse my noble father laid on thee,

177.play: When thou didst crown his warlike brows with paper

178.play: And with thy scorns drew'st rivers from his eyes,

179.play: And then, to dry them, gavest the duke a clout

180.play: Steep'd in the faultless blood of pretty Rutland--

181.play: His curses, then from bitterness of soul

182.play: Denounced against thee, are all fall'n upon thee;

183.play: And God, not we, hath plagued thy bloody deed.

184.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: So just is God, to right the innocent.

185.play: HASTINGS: O, 'twas the foulest deed to slay that babe,

186.play: And the most merciless that e'er was heard of!

187.play: RIVERS: Tyrants themselves wept when it was reported.

188.play: DORSET: No man but prophesied revenge for it.

189.play: BUCKINGHAM: Northumberland, then present, wept to see it.

190.play: QUEEN MARGARET: What were you snarling all before I came,

191.play: Ready to catch each other by the throat,

192.play: And turn you all your hatred now on me?

193.play: Did York's dread curse prevail so much with heaven?

194.play: That Henry's death, my lovely Edward's death,

195.play: Their kingdom's loss, my woful banishment,

196.play: Could all but answer for that peevish brat?

197.play: Can curses pierce the clouds and enter heaven?

198.play: Why, then, give way, dull clouds, to my quick curses!

199.play: If not by war, by surfeit die your king,

200.play: As ours by murder, to make him a king!

201.play: Edward thy son, which now is Prince of Wales,

202.play: For Edward my son, which was Prince of Wales,

203.play: Die in his youth by like untimely violence!

204.play: Thyself a queen, for me that was a queen,

205.play: Outlive thy glory, like my wretched self!

206.play: Long mayst thou live to wail thy children's loss;

207.play: And see another, as I see thee now,

208.play: Deck'd in thy rights, as thou art stall'd in mine!

209.play: Long die thy happy days before thy death;

210.play: And, after many lengthen'd hours of grief,

211.play: Die neither mother, wife, nor England's queen!

212.play: Rivers and Dorset, you were standers by,

213.play: And so wast thou, Lord Hastings, when my son

214.play: Was stabb'd with bloody daggers: God, I pray him,

215.play: That none of you may live your natural age,

216.play: But by some unlook'd accident cut off!

217.play: GLOUCESTER: Have done thy charm, thou hateful wither'd hag!

218.play: QUEEN MARGARET: And leave out thee? stay, dog, for thou shalt hear me.

219.play: If heaven have any grievous plague in store

220.play: Exceeding those that I can wish upon thee,

221.play: O, let them keep it till thy sins be ripe,

222.play: And then hurl down their indignation

223.play: On thee, the troubler of the poor world's peace!

224.play: The worm of conscience still begnaw thy soul!

225.play: Thy friends suspect for traitors while thou livest,

226.play: And take deep traitors for thy dearest friends!

227.play: No sleep close up that deadly eye of thine,

228.play: Unless it be whilst some tormenting dream

229.play: Affrights thee with a hell of ugly devils!

231.play: Thou that wast seal'd in thy nativity

232.play: The slave of nature and the son of hell!

233.play: Thou slander of thy mother's heavy womb!

234.play: Thou loathed issue of thy father's loins!

235.play: Thou rag of honour! thou detested--

239.play: QUEEN MARGARET: I call thee not.

240.play: GLOUCESTER: I cry thee mercy then, for I had thought

241.play: That thou hadst call'd me all these bitter names.

242.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Why, so I did; but look'd for no reply.

243.play: O, let me make the period to my curse!

244.play: GLOUCESTER: 'Tis done by me, and ends in 'Margaret.'

245.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Thus have you breathed your curse against yourself.

246.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Poor painted queen, vain flourish of my fortune!

247.play: Why strew'st thou sugar on that bottled spider,

248.play: Whose deadly web ensnareth thee about?

249.play: Fool, fool! thou whet'st a knife to kill thyself.

250.play: The time will come when thou shalt wish for me

251.play: To help thee curse that poisonous bunchback'd toad.

252.play: HASTINGS: False-boding woman, end thy frantic curse,

253.play: Lest to thy harm thou move our patience.

254.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Foul shame upon you! you have all moved mine.

255.play: RIVERS: Were you well served, you would be taught your duty.

256.play: QUEEN MARGARET: To serve me well, you all should do me duty,

257.play: Teach me to be your queen, and you my subjects:

258.play: O, serve me well, and teach yourselves that duty!

259.play: DORSET: Dispute not with her; she is lunatic.

260.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Peace, master marquess, you are malapert:

261.play: Your fire-new stamp of honour is scarce current.

262.play: O, that your young nobility could judge

263.play: What 'twere to lose it, and be miserable!

264.play: They that stand high have many blasts to shake them;

265.play: And if they fall, they dash themselves to pieces.

266.play: GLOUCESTER: Good counsel, marry: learn it, learn it, marquess.

267.play: DORSET: It toucheth you, my lord, as much as me.

268.play: GLOUCESTER: Yea, and much more: but I was born so high,

269.play: Our aery buildeth in the cedar's top,

270.play: And dallies with the wind and scorns the sun.

271.play: QUEEN MARGARET: And turns the sun to shade; alas! alas!

272.play: Witness my son, now in the shade of death;

273.play: Whose bright out-shining beams thy cloudy wrath

274.play: Hath in eternal darkness folded up.

275.play: Your aery buildeth in our aery's nest.

276.play: O God, that seest it, do not suffer it!

277.play: As it was won with blood, lost be it so!

278.play: BUCKINGHAM: Have done! for shame, if not for charity.

279.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Urge neither charity nor shame to me:

280.play: Uncharitably with me have you dealt,

281.play: And shamefully by you my hopes are butcher'd.

282.play: My charity is outrage, life my shame

283.play: And in that shame still live my sorrow's rage.

285.play: QUEEN MARGARET: O princely Buckingham I'll kiss thy hand,

286.play: In sign of league and amity with thee:

287.play: Now fair befal thee and thy noble house!

288.play: Thy garments are not spotted with our blood,

289.play: Nor thou within the compass of my curse.

290.play: BUCKINGHAM: Nor no one here; for curses never pass

291.play: The lips of those that breathe them in the air.

292.play: QUEEN MARGARET: I'll not believe but they ascend the sky,

293.play: And there awake God's gentle-sleeping peace.

294.play: O Buckingham, take heed of yonder dog!

295.play: Look, when he fawns, he bites; and when he bites,

296.play: His venom tooth will rankle to the death:

297.play: Have not to do with him, beware of him;

298.play: Sin, death, and hell have set their marks on him,

299.play: And all their ministers attend on him.

300.play: GLOUCESTER: What doth she say, my Lord of Buckingham?

301.play: BUCKINGHAM: Nothing that I respect, my gracious lord.

302.play: QUEEN MARGARET: What, dost thou scorn me for my gentle counsel?

303.play: And soothe the devil that I warn thee from?

304.play: O, but remember this another day,

305.play: When he shall split thy very heart with sorrow,

306.play: And say poor Margaret was a prophetess!

307.play: Live each of you the subjects to his hate,

308.play: And he to yours, and all of you to God's!

309.play: HASTINGS: My hair doth stand on end to hear her curses.

310.play: RIVERS: And so doth mine: I muse why she's at liberty.

311.play: GLOUCESTER: I cannot blame her: by God's holy mother,

312.play: She hath had too much wrong; and I repent

313.play: My part thereof that I have done to her.

314.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: I never did her any, to my knowledge.

315.play: GLOUCESTER: But you have all the vantage of her wrong.

316.play: I was too hot to do somebody good,

317.play: That is too cold in thinking of it now.

318.play: Marry, as for Clarence, he is well repaid,

319.play: He is frank'd up to fatting for his pains

320.play: God pardon them that are the cause of it!

321.play: RIVERS: A virtuous and a Christian-like conclusion,

322.play: To pray for them that have done scathe to us.

325.play: For had I cursed now, I had cursed myself.

326.play: CATESBY: Madam, his majesty doth call for you,

327.play: And for your grace; and you, my noble lords.

328.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Catesby, we come. Lords, will you go with us?

329.play: RIVERS: Madam, we will attend your grace.

330.play: GLOUCESTER: I do the wrong, and first begin to brawl.

331.play: The secret mischiefs that I set abroach

332.play: I lay unto the grievous charge of others.

333.play: Clarence, whom I, indeed, have laid in darkness,

334.play: I do beweep to many simple gulls

336.play: And say it is the queen and her allies

337.play: That stir the king against the duke my brother.

338.play: Now, they believe it; and withal whet me

339.play: To be revenged on Rivers, Vaughan, Grey:

340.play: But then I sigh; and, with a piece of scripture,

341.play: Tell them that God bids us do good for evil:

342.play: And thus I clothe my naked villany

343.play: With old odd ends stolen out of holy writ;

344.play: And seem a saint, when most I play the devil.

345.play: But, soft! here come my executioners.

346.play: How now, my hardy, stout resolved mates!

347.play: Are you now going to dispatch this deed?

348.play: Well thought upon; I have it here about me.

349.play: When you have done, repair to Crosby Place.

350.play: But, sirs, be sudden in the execution,

351.play: Withal obdurate, do not hear him plead;

352.play: For Clarence is well-spoken, and perhaps

353.play: May move your hearts to pity if you mark him.

354.play: Your eyes drop millstones, when fools' eyes drop tears:

355.play: I like you, lads; about your business straight;

1.play: Enter CLARENCE and BRAKENBURY

2.play: BRAKENBURY: Why looks your grace so heavily today?

3.play: CLARENCE: O, I have pass'd a miserable night,

4.play: So full of ugly sights, of ghastly dreams,

5.play: That, as I am a Christian faithful man,

6.play: I would not spend another such a night,

7.play: Though 'twere to buy a world of happy days,

8.play: So full of dismal terror was the time!

9.play: BRAKENBURY: What was your dream? I long to hear you tell it.

10.play: CLARENCE: Methoughts that I had broken from the Tower,

11.play: And was embark'd to cross to Burgundy;

12.play: And, in my company, my brother Gloucester;

13.play: Who from my cabin tempted me to walk

14.play: Upon the hatches: thence we looked toward England,

15.play: And cited up a thousand fearful times,

16.play: During the wars of York and Lancaster

17.play: That had befall'n us. As we paced along

18.play: Upon the giddy footing of the hatches,

19.play: Methought that Gloucester stumbled; and, in falling,

20.play: Struck me, that thought to stay him, overboard,

21.play: Into the tumbling billows of the main.

22.play: Lord, Lord! methought, what pain it was to drown!

23.play: What dreadful noise of waters in mine ears!

24.play: What ugly sights of death within mine eyes!

25.play: Methought I saw a thousand fearful wrecks;

26.play: Ten thousand men that fishes gnaw'd upon;

27.play: Wedges of gold, great anchors, heaps of pearl,

28.play: Inestimable stones, unvalued jewels,

29.play: All scatter'd in the bottom of the sea:

30.play: Some lay in dead men's skulls; and, in those holes

31.play: Where eyes did once inhabit, there were crept,

32.play: As 'twere in scorn of eyes, reflecting gems,

33.play: Which woo'd the slimy bottom of the deep,

34.play: And mock'd the dead bones that lay scatter'd by.

35.play: BRAKENBURY: Had you such leisure in the time of death

36.play: To gaze upon the secrets of the deep?

37.play: CLARENCE: Methought I had; and often did I strive

38.play: To yield the ghost: but still the envious flood

39.play: Kept in my soul, and would not let it forth

40.play: To seek the empty, vast and wandering air;

41.play: But smother'd it within my panting bulk,

42.play: Which almost burst to belch it in the sea.

43.play: BRAKENBURY: Awaked you not with this sore agony?

44.play: CLARENCE: O, no, my dream was lengthen'd after life;

45.play: O, then began the tempest to my soul,

46.play: Who pass'd, methought, the melancholy flood,

47.play: With that grim ferryman which poets write of,

48.play: Unto the kingdom of perpetual night.

49.play: The first that there did greet my stranger soul,

50.play: Was my great father-in-law, renowned Warwick;

51.play: Who cried aloud, 'What scourge for perjury

52.play: Can this dark monarchy afford false Clarence?'

53.play: And so he vanish'd: then came wandering by

54.play: A shadow like an angel, with bright hair

55.play: Dabbled in blood; and he squeak'd out aloud,

56.play: 'Clarence is come; false, fleeting, perjured Clarence,

57.play: That stabb'd me in the field by Tewksbury;

58.play: Seize on him, Furies, take him to your torments!'

59.play: With that, methoughts, a legion of foul fiends

60.play: Environ'd me about, and howled in mine ears

61.play: Such hideous cries, that with the very noise

62.play: I trembling waked, and for a season after

63.play: Could not believe but that I was in hell,

64.play: Such terrible impression made the dream.

65.play: BRAKENBURY: No marvel, my lord, though it affrighted you;

66.play: I promise, I am afraid to hear you tell it.

67.play: CLARENCE: O Brakenbury, I have done those things,

68.play: Which now bear evidence against my soul,

69.play: For Edward's sake; and see how he requites me!

70.play: O God! if my deep prayers cannot appease thee,

71.play: But thou wilt be avenged on my misdeeds,

72.play: Yet execute thy wrath in me alone,

73.play: O, spare my guiltless wife and my poor children!

74.play: I pray thee, gentle keeper, stay by me;

75.play: My soul is heavy, and I fain would sleep.

76.play: BRAKENBURY: I will, my lord: God give your grace good rest!

77.play: Sorrow breaks seasons and reposing hours,

78.play: Makes the night morning, and the noon-tide night.

79.play: Princes have but their tides for their glories,

80.play: An outward honour for an inward toil;

82.play: They often feel a world of restless cares:

83.play: So that, betwixt their tides and low names,

84.play: There's nothing differs but the outward fame.

85.play: In God's name what are you, and how came you hither?

87.play: I am, in this, commanded to deliver

88.play: The noble Duke of Clarence to your hands:

89.play: I will not reason what is meant hereby,

90.play: Because I will be guiltless of the meaning.

91.play: Here are the keys, there sits the duke asleep:

92.play: I'll to the king; and signify to him

93.play: That thus I have resign'd my charge to you.

95.play: CLARENCE: Where art thou, keeper? give me a cup of wine.

96.play: In God's name, what art thou?

97.play: But not, as I am, royal.

98.play: Thy voice is thunder, but thy looks are humble.

99.play: How darkly and how deadly dost thou speak!

100.play: Your eyes do menace me: why look you pale?

101.play: Who sent you hither? Wherefore do you come?

103.play: You scarcely have the hearts to tell me so,

104.play: And therefore cannot have the hearts to do it.

105.play: Wherein, my friends, have I offended you?

106.play: I shall be reconciled to him again.

107.play: Are you call'd forth from out a world of men

108.play: To slay the innocent? What is my offence?

109.play: Where are the evidence that do accuse me?

110.play: What lawful quest have given their verdict up

111.play: Unto the frowning judge? or who pronounced

112.play: The bitter sentence of poor Clarence' death?

113.play: Before I be convict by course of law,

114.play: To threaten me with death is most unlawful.

115.play: I charge you, as you hope to have redemption

116.play: By Christ's dear blood shed for our grievous sins,

117.play: That you depart and lay no hands on me

118.play: The deed you undertake is damnable.

119.play: Erroneous vassal! the great King of kings

120.play: Hath in the tables of his law commanded

121.play: That thou shalt do no murder: and wilt thou, then,

122.play: Spurn at his edict and fulfil a man's?

123.play: Take heed; for he holds vengeance in his hands,

124.play: To hurl upon their heads that break his law.

125.play: Alas! for whose sake did I that ill deed?

126.play: For Edward, for my brother, for his sake: Why, sirs,

127.play: He sends ye not to murder me for this

128.play: For in this sin he is as deep as I.

129.play: If God will be revenged for this deed.

130.play: O, know you yet, he doth it publicly,

131.play: Take not the quarrel from his powerful arm;

132.play: He needs no indirect nor lawless course

133.play: To cut off those that have offended him.

134.play: My brother's love, the devil, and my rage.

135.play: Oh, if you love my brother, hate not me;

136.play: I am his brother, and I love him well.

137.play: If you be hired for meed, go back again,

138.play: And I will send you to my brother Gloucester,

139.play: Who shall reward you better for my life

140.play: Than Edward will for tidings of my death.

141.play: O, no, he loves me, and he holds me dear:

142.play: Go you to him from me.

143.play: Tell him, when that our princely father York

144.play: Bless'd his three sons with his victorious arm,

145.play: And charged us from his soul to love each other,

146.play: He little thought of this divided friendship:

147.play: Bid Gloucester think of this, and he will weep.

148.play: O, do not slander him, for he is kind.

149.play: It cannot be; for when I parted with him,

150.play: He hugg'd me in his arms, and swore, with sobs,

151.play: That he would labour my delivery.

152.play: Hast thou that holy feeling in thy soul,

153.play: To counsel me to make my peace with God,

154.play: And art thou yet to thy own soul so blind,

155.play: That thou wilt war with God by murdering me?

156.play: Ah, sirs, consider, he that set you on

157.play: To do this deed will hate you for the deed.

159.play: Not to relent is beastly, savage, devilish.

160.play: Which of you, if you were a prince's son,

161.play: Being pent from liberty, as I am now,

162.play: if two such murderers as yourselves came to you,

164.play: My friend, I spy some pity in thy looks:

165.play: O, if thine eye be not a flatterer,

166.play: Come thou on my side, and entreat for me,

167.play: As you would beg, were you in my distress

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING EDWARD IV sick, QUEEN  ELIZABETH, DORSET, RIVERS, HASTINGS, BUCKINGHAM, GREY, and others

2.play: KING EDWARD IV: Why, so: now have I done a good day's work:

3.play: You peers, continue this united league:

4.play: I every day expect an embassage

5.play: From my Redeemer to redeem me hence;

6.play: And now in peace my soul shall part to heaven,

7.play: Since I have set my friends at peace on earth.

8.play: Rivers and Hastings, take each other's hand;

9.play: Dissemble not your hatred, swear your love.

10.play: RIVERS: By heaven, my heart is purged from grudging hate:

11.play: And with my hand I seal my true heart's love.

12.play: HASTINGS: So thrive I, as I truly swear the like!

13.play: KING EDWARD IV: Take heed you dally not before your king;

14.play: Lest he that is the supreme King of kings

15.play: Confound your hidden falsehood, and award

16.play: Either of you to be the other's end.

17.play: HASTINGS: So prosper I, as I swear perfect love!

18.play: RIVERS: And I, as I love Hastings with my heart!

19.play: KING EDWARD IV: Madam, yourself are not exempt in this,

20.play: Nor your son Dorset, Buckingham, nor you;

21.play: You have been factious one against the other,

22.play: Wife, love Lord Hastings, let him kiss your hand;

23.play: And what you do, do it unfeignedly.

24.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Here, Hastings; I will never more remember

25.play: Our former hatred, so thrive I and mine!

26.play: KING EDWARD IV: Dorset, embrace him; Hastings, love lord marquess.

27.play: DORSET: This interchange of love, I here protest,

28.play: Upon my part shall be unviolable.

29.play: HASTINGS: And so swear I, my lord

30.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now, princely Buckingham, seal thou this league

31.play: With thy embracements to my wife's allies,

32.play: And make me happy in your unity.

33.play: BUCKINGHAM: Whenever Buckingham doth turn his hate

36.play: Doth cherish you and yours, God punish me

37.play: With hate in those where I expect most love!

38.play: When I have most need to employ a friend,

39.play: And most assured that he is a friend

40.play: Deep, hollow, treacherous, and full of guile,

41.play: Be he unto me! this do I beg of God,

42.play: When I am cold in zeal to yours.

43.play: KING EDWARD IV: A pleasing cordial, princely Buckingham,

44.play: is this thy vow unto my sickly heart.

45.play: There wanteth now our brother Gloucester here,

46.play: To make the perfect period of this peace.

47.play: BUCKINGHAM: And, in good time, here comes the noble duke.

48.play: GLOUCESTER: Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen:

49.play: And, princely peers, a happy time of day!

50.play: KING EDWARD IV: Happy, indeed, as we have spent the day.

51.play: Brother, we done deeds of charity;

52.play: Made peace enmity, fair love of hate,

54.play: GLOUCESTER: A blessed labour, my most sovereign liege:

55.play: Amongst this princely heap, if any here,

56.play: By false intelligence, or wrong surmise,

58.play: If I unwittingly, or in my rage,

59.play: Have aught committed that is hardly borne

60.play: By any in this presence, I desire

61.play: To reconcile me to his friendly peace:

62.play: 'Tis death to me to be at enmity;

63.play: I hate it, and desire all good men's love.

64.play: First, madam, I entreat true peace of you,

65.play: Which I will purchase with my duteous service;

66.play: Of you, my noble cousin Buckingham,

67.play: If ever any grudge were lodged between us;

68.play: Of you, Lord Rivers, and, Lord Grey, of you;

69.play: That without desert have frown'd on me;

70.play: Dukes, earls, lords, gentlemen; indeed, of all.

71.play: I do not know that Englishman alive

72.play: With whom my soul is any jot at odds

73.play: More than the infant that is born to-night

74.play: I thank my God for my humility.

75.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: A holy day shall this be kept hereafter:

76.play: I would to God all strifes were well compounded.

77.play: My sovereign liege, I do beseech your majesty

78.play: To take our brother Clarence to your grace.

79.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, madam, have I offer'd love for this

80.play: To be so bouted in this royal presence?

81.play: Who knows not that the noble duke is dead?

82.play: You do him injury to scorn his corse.

83.play: RIVERS: Who knows not he is dead! who knows he is?

84.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: All seeing heaven, what a world is this!

85.play: BUCKINGHAM: Look I so pale, Lord Dorset, as the rest?

86.play: DORSET: Ay, my good lord; and no one in this presence

87.play: But his red colour hath forsook his cheeks.

88.play: KING EDWARD IV: Is Clarence dead? the order was reversed.

89.play: GLOUCESTER: But he, poor soul, by your first order died,

90.play: And that a winged Mercury did bear:

91.play: Some tardy cripple bore the countermand,

92.play: That came too lag to see him buried.

93.play: God grant that some, less noble and less loyal,

94.play: Nearer in bloody thoughts, but not in blood,

95.play: Deserve not worse than wretched Clarence did,

96.play: And yet go current from suspicion!

97.play: DERBY: A boon, my sovereign, for my service done!

98.play: KING EDWARD IV: I pray thee, peace: my soul is full of sorrow.

99.play: DERBY: I will not rise, unless your highness grant.

100.play: KING EDWARD IV: Then speak at once what is it thou demand'st.

101.play: DERBY: The forfeit, sovereign, of my servant's life;

102.play: Who slew to-day a righteous gentleman

103.play: Lately attendant on the Duke of Norfolk.

104.play: KING EDWARD IV: Have a tongue to doom my brother's death,

105.play: And shall the same give pardon to a slave?

106.play: My brother slew no man; his fault was thought,

107.play: And yet his punishment was cruel death.

108.play: Who sued to me for him? who, in my rage,

109.play: Kneel'd at my feet, and bade me be advised

110.play: Who spake of brotherhood? who spake of love?

111.play: Who told me how the poor soul did forsake

112.play: The mighty Warwick, and did fight for me?

113.play: Who told me, in the field by Tewksbury

114.play: When Oxford had me down, he rescued me,

115.play: And said, 'Dear brother, live, and be a king'?

116.play: Who told me, when we both lay in the field

117.play: Frozen almost to death, how he did lap me

118.play: Even in his own garments, and gave himself,

119.play: All thin and naked, to the numb cold night?

120.play: All this from my remembrance brutish wrath

121.play: Sinfully pluck'd, and not a man of you

122.play: Had so much grace to put it in my mind.

123.play: But when your carters or your waiting-vassals

124.play: Have done a drunken slaughter, and defaced

125.play: The precious image of our dear Redeemer,

126.play: You straight are on your knees for pardon, pardon;

127.play: And I unjustly too, must grant it you

128.play: But for my brother not a man would speak,

129.play: Nor I, ungracious, speak unto myself

130.play: For him, poor soul. The proudest of you all

131.play: Have been beholding to him in his life;

132.play: Yet none of you would once plead for his life.

133.play: O God, I fear thy justice will take hold

134.play: On me, and you, and mine, and yours for this!

135.play: Come, Hastings, help me to my closet.

137.play: GLOUCESTER: This is the fruit of rashness! Mark'd you not

138.play: How that the guilty kindred of the queen

139.play: Look'd pale when they did hear of Clarence' death?

140.play: O, they did urge it still unto the king!

141.play: God will revenge it. But come, let us in,

142.play: To comfort Edward with our company.

143.play: BUCKINGHAM: We wait upon your grace.

1.play: Enter the DUCHESS OF YORK, with the two children of CLARENCE

2.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: No, boy.

3.play: My pretty cousins, you mistake me much;

4.play: I do lament the sickness of the king.

5.play: As loath to lose him, not your father's death;

6.play: It were lost sorrow to wail one that's lost.

7.play: Peace, children, peace! the king doth love you well:

8.play: Incapable and shallow innocents,

9.play: You cannot guess who caused your father's death.

10.play: Oh, that deceit should steal such gentle shapes,

11.play: And with a virtuous vizard hide foul guile!

12.play: He is my son; yea, and therein my shame;

13.play: Yet from my dugs he drew not this deceit.

14.play: Ay, boy.

15.play: Enter QUEEN ELIZABETH, with her hair about her ears; RIVERS, and DORSET after her

16.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Oh, who shall hinder me to wail and weep,

17.play: To chide my fortune, and torment myself?

18.play: I'll join with black despair against my soul,

19.play: And to myself become an enemy.

20.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: What means this scene of rude impatience?

21.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: To make an act of tragic violence:

22.play: Edward, my lord, your son, our king, is dead.

23.play: Why grow the branches now the root is wither'd?

24.play: Why wither not the leaves the sap being gone?

25.play: If you will live, lament; if die, be brief,

26.play: That our swift-winged souls may catch the king's;

27.play: Or, like obedient subjects, follow him

28.play: To his new kingdom of perpetual rest.

29.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Ah, so much interest have I in thy sorrow

30.play: As I had title in thy noble husband!

31.play: I have bewept a worthy husband's death,

32.play: And lived by looking on his images:

33.play: But now two mirrors of his princely semblance

34.play: Are crack'd in pieces by malignant death,

35.play: And I for comfort have but one false glass,

36.play: Which grieves me when I see my shame in him.

37.play: Thou art a widow; yet thou art a mother,

38.play: And hast the comfort of thy children left thee:

39.play: But death hath snatch'd my husband from mine arms,

40.play: And pluck'd two crutches from my feeble limbs,

41.play: Edward and Clarence. O, what cause have I,

42.play: Thine being but a moiety of my grief,

43.play: To overgo thy plaints and drown thy cries!

44.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Give me no help in lamentation;

45.play: I am not barren to bring forth complaints

46.play: All springs reduce their currents to mine eyes,

47.play: That I, being govern'd by the watery moon,

48.play: May send forth plenteous tears to drown the world!

49.play: Oh for my husband, for my dear lord Edward!

50.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Alas for both, both mine, Edward and Clarence!

51.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: What stay had I but Edward? and he's gone.

52.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: What stays had I but they? and they are gone.

53.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Was never widow had so dear a loss!

54.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Was never mother had so dear a loss!

55.play: Alas, I am the mother of these moans!

56.play: Their woes are parcell'd, mine are general.

57.play: She for an Edward weeps, and so do I;

58.play: I for a Clarence weep, so doth not she:

59.play: These babes for Clarence weep and so do I;

60.play: I for an Edward weep, so do not they:

61.play: Alas, you three, on me, threefold distress'd,

62.play: Pour all your tears! I am your sorrow's nurse,

63.play: And I will pamper it with lamentations.

64.play: DORSET: Comfort, dear mother: God is much displeased

65.play: That you take with unthankfulness, his doing:

66.play: In common worldly things, 'tis call'd ungrateful,

67.play: With dull unwilligness to repay a debt

68.play: Which with a bounteous hand was kindly lent;

69.play: Much more to be thus opposite with heaven,

70.play: For it requires the royal debt it lent you.

71.play: RIVERS: Madam, bethink you, like a careful mother,

72.play: Of the young prince your son: send straight for him

73.play: Let him be crown'd; in him your comfort lives:

74.play: Drown desperate sorrow in dead Edward's grave,

75.play: And plant your joys in living Edward's throne.

76.play: GLOUCESTER: Madam, have comfort: all of us have cause

77.play: To wail the dimming of our shining star;

78.play: But none can cure their harms by wailing them.

79.play: Madam, my mother, I do cry you mercy;

80.play: I did not see your grace: humbly on my knee

82.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: God bless thee; and put meekness in thy mind,

83.play: Love, charity, obedience, and true duty!

84.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside]  Amen; and make me die a good old man!

85.play: That is the butt-end of a mother's blessing:

86.play: I marvel why her grace did leave it out.

87.play: BUCKINGHAM: You cloudy princes and heart-sorrowing peers,

88.play: That bear this mutual heavy load of moan,

89.play: Now cheer each other in each other's love

90.play: Though we have spent our harvest of this king,

91.play: We are to reap the harvest of his son.

92.play: The broken rancour of your high-swoln hearts,

93.play: But lately splinter'd, knit, and join'd together,

94.play: Must gently be preserved, cherish'd, and kept:

95.play: Me seemeth good, that, with some little train,

96.play: Forthwith from Ludlow the young prince be fetch'd

97.play: Hither to London, to be crown'd our king.

98.play: RIVERS: Why with some little train, my Lord of Buckingham?

99.play: BUCKINGHAM: Marry, my lord, lest, by a multitude,

100.play: The new-heal'd wound of malice should break out,

101.play: Which would be so much the more dangerous

102.play: By how much the estate is green and yet ungovern'd:

103.play: Where every horse bears his commanding rein,

104.play: And may direct his course as please himself,

105.play: As well the fear of harm, as harm apparent,

106.play: In my opinion, ought to be prevented.

107.play: GLOUCESTER: I hope the king made peace with all of us

108.play: And the compact is firm and true in me.

109.play: RIVERS: And so in me; and so, I think, in all:

110.play: Yet, since it is but green, it should be put

111.play: To no apparent likelihood of breach,

112.play: Which haply by much company might be urged:

113.play: Therefore I say with noble Buckingham,

114.play: That it is meet so few should fetch the prince.

116.play: GLOUCESTER: Then be it so; and go we to determine

117.play: Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow.

118.play: Madam, and you, my mother, will you go

119.play: To give your censures in this weighty business?

120.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: With all our harts.

121.play: BUCKINGHAM: My lord, whoever journeys to the Prince,

122.play: For God's sake, let not us two be behind;

123.play: For, by the way, I'll sort occasion,

124.play: As index to the story we late talk'd of,

125.play: To part the queen's proud kindred from the king.

126.play: GLOUCESTER: My other self, my counsel's consistory,

127.play: My oracle, my prophet! My dear cousin,

128.play: I, like a child, will go by thy direction.

129.play: Towards Ludlow then, for we'll not stay behind.

1.play: Enter the ARCHBISHOP OF YORK, young YORK, QUEEN ELIZABETH, and the DUCHESS OF YORK

3.play: At Stony-Stratford will they be to-night:

4.play: To-morrow, or next day, they will be here.

6.play: I hope he is much grown since last I saw him.

7.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But I hear, no; they say my son of York

8.play: Hath almost overta'en him in his growth.

9.play: YORK: Ay, mother; but I would not have it so.

10.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Why, my young cousin, it is good to grow.

11.play: YORK: Grandam, one night, as we did sit at supper,

12.play: My uncle Rivers talk'd how I did grow

13.play: More than my brother: 'Ay,' quoth my uncle

14.play: Gloucester,

15.play: 'Small herbs have grace, great weeds do grow apace:'

16.play: And since, methinks, I would not grow so fast,

17.play: Because sweet flowers are slow and weeds make haste.

18.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Good faith, good faith, the saying did not hold

19.play: In him that did object the same to thee;

20.play: He was the wretched'st thing when he was young,

21.play: So long a-growing and so leisurely,

22.play: That, if this rule were true, he should be gracious.

23.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Why, madam, so, no doubt, he is.

25.play: YORK: Now, by my troth, if I had been remember'd,

26.play: I could have given my uncle's grace a flout,

27.play: To touch his growth nearer than he touch'd mine.

28.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: How, my pretty York? I pray thee, let me hear it.

29.play: YORK: Marry, they say my uncle grew so fast

30.play: That he could gnaw a crust at two hours old

31.play: 'Twas full two years ere I could get a tooth.

32.play: Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.

33.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I pray thee, pretty York, who told thee this?

35.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: His nurse! why, she was dead ere thou wert born.

36.play: YORK: If 'twere not she, I cannot tell who told me.

37.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: A parlous boy: go to, you are too shrewd.

38.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Good madam, be not angry with the child.

40.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Here comes a messenger. What news?

41.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: How fares the prince?

42.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: What is thy news then?

45.play: Ay me, I see the downfall of our house!

46.play: The tiger now hath seized the gentle hind;

48.play: Upon the innocent and aweless throne:

50.play: I see, as in a map, the end of all.

51.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Accursed and unquiet wrangling days,

52.play: How many of you have mine eyes beheld!

53.play: My husband lost his life to get the crown;

54.play: And often up and down my sons were toss'd,

55.play: For me to joy and weep their gain and loss:

56.play: And being seated, and domestic broils

58.play: Make war upon themselves; blood against blood,

1.play: BUCKINGHAM: Welcome, sweet prince, to London, to your chamber.

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts' sovereign

3.play: The weary way hath made you melancholy.

4.play: PRINCE EDWARD: No, uncle; but our crosses on the way

5.play: Have made it tedious, wearisome, and heavy

6.play: I want more uncles here to welcome me.

7.play: GLOUCESTER: Sweet prince, the untainted virtue of your years

8.play: Hath not yet dived into the world's deceit

9.play: Nor more can you distinguish of a man

10.play: Than of his outward show; which, God he knows,

11.play: Seldom or never jumpeth with the heart.

12.play: Those uncles which you want were dangerous;

13.play: Your grace attended to their sugar'd words,

14.play: But look'd not on the poison of their hearts :

15.play: God keep you from them, and from such false friends!

16.play: PRINCE EDWARD: God keep me from false friends! but they were none.

17.play: GLOUCESTER: My lord, the mayor of London comes to greet you.

18.play: PRINCE EDWARD: I thank you, good my lord; and thank you all.

19.play: I thought my mother, and my brother York,

20.play: Would long ere this have met us on the way

21.play: Fie, what a slug is Hastings, that he comes not

22.play: To tell us whether they will come or no!

23.play: BUCKINGHAM: And, in good time, here comes the sweating lord.

24.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Welcome, my lord: what, will our mother come?

25.play: HASTINGS: On what occasion, God he knows, not I,

26.play: The queen your mother, and your brother York,

27.play: Have taken sanctuary: the tender prince

28.play: Would fain have come with me to meet your grace,

29.play: But by his mother was perforce withheld.

30.play: BUCKINGHAM: Fie, what an indirect and peevish course

31.play: Is this of hers! Lord cardinal, will your grace

32.play: Persuade the queen to send the Duke of York

34.play: If she deny, Lord Hastings, go with him,

35.play: And from her jealous arms pluck him perforce.

36.play: CARDINAL: My Lord of Buckingham, if my weak oratory

37.play: Can from his mother win the Duke of York,

38.play: Anon expect him here; but if she be obdurate

39.play: To mild entreaties, God in heaven forbid

40.play: We should infringe the holy privilege

41.play: Of blessed sanctuary! not for all this land

42.play: Would I be guilty of so deep a sin.

43.play: BUCKINGHAM: You are too senseless--obstinate, my lord,

45.play: Weigh it but with the grossness of this age,

46.play: You break not sanctuary in seizing him.

47.play: The benefit thereof is always granted

48.play: To those whose dealings have deserved the place,

49.play: And those who have the wit to claim the place:

50.play: This prince hath neither claim'd it nor deserved it;

51.play: And therefore, in mine opinion, cannot have it:

52.play: Then, taking him from thence that is not there,

53.play: You break no privilege nor charter there.

60.play: Say, uncle Gloucester, if our brother come,

62.play: GLOUCESTER: Where it seems best unto your royal self.

63.play: If I may counsel you, some day or two
