83.play: I moralize two meanings in one word.

84.play: PRINCE EDWARD: That Julius Caesar was a famous man;

85.play: With what his valour did enrich his wit,

86.play: His wit set down to make his valour live

87.play: Death makes no conquest of this conqueror;

88.play: For now he lives in fame, though not in life.

89.play: I'll tell you what, my cousin Buckingham,--

91.play: PRINCE EDWARD: An if I live until I be a man,

92.play: I'll win our ancient right in France again,

93.play: Or die a soldier, as I lived a king.

94.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside]  Short summers lightly have a forward spring.

95.play: BUCKINGHAM: Now, in good time, here comes the Duke of York.

96.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Richard of York! how fares our loving brother?

97.play: YORK: Well, my dread lord; so must I call you now.

98.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Ay, brother, to our grief, as it is yours:

99.play: Too late he died that might have kept that title,

100.play: Which by his death hath lost much majesty.

101.play: GLOUCESTER: How fares our cousin, noble Lord of York?

102.play: YORK: I thank you, gentle uncle. O, my lord,

103.play: You said that idle weeds are fast in growth

104.play: The prince my brother hath outgrown me far.

106.play: YORK: And therefore is he idle?

107.play: GLOUCESTER: O, my fair cousin, I must not say so.

108.play: YORK: Then is he more beholding to you than I.

109.play: GLOUCESTER: He may command me as my sovereign;

110.play: But you have power in me as in a kinsman.

111.play: YORK: I pray you, uncle, give me this dagger.

112.play: GLOUCESTER: My dagger, little cousin? with all my heart.

114.play: YORK: Of my kind uncle, that I know will give;

115.play: And being but a toy, which is no grief to give.

116.play: GLOUCESTER: A greater gift than that I'll give my cousin.

117.play: YORK: A greater gift! O, that's the sword to it.

118.play: GLOUCESTER: A gentle cousin, were it light enough.

119.play: YORK: O, then, I see, you will part but with light gifts;

120.play: In weightier things you'll say a beggar nay.

121.play: GLOUCESTER: It is too heavy for your grace to wear.

122.play: YORK: I weigh it lightly, were it heavier.

123.play: GLOUCESTER: What, would you have my weapon, little lord?

124.play: YORK: I would, that I might thank you as you call me.

127.play: PRINCE EDWARD: My Lord of York will still be cross in talk:

128.play: Uncle, your grace knows how to bear with him.

129.play: YORK: You mean, to bear me, not to bear with me:

130.play: Uncle, my brother mocks both you and me;

131.play: Because that I am little, like an ape,

132.play: He thinks that you should bear me on your shoulders.

133.play: BUCKINGHAM: With what a sharp-provided wit he reasons!

134.play: To mitigate the scorn he gives his uncle,

135.play: He prettily and aptly taunts himself:

136.play: So cunning and so young is wonderful.

137.play: GLOUCESTER: My lord, will't please you pass along?

138.play: Myself and my good cousin Buckingham

139.play: Will to your mother, to entreat of her

140.play: To meet you at the Tower and welcome you.

141.play: YORK: What, will you go unto the Tower, my lord?

142.play: PRINCE EDWARD: My lord protector needs will have it so.

143.play: YORK: I shall not sleep in quiet at the Tower.

144.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, what should you fear?

145.play: YORK: Marry, my uncle Clarence' angry ghost:

146.play: My grandam told me he was murdered there.

147.play: PRINCE EDWARD: I fear no uncles dead.

148.play: GLOUCESTER: Nor none that live, I hope.

149.play: PRINCE EDWARD: An if they live, I hope I need not fear.

150.play: But come, my lord; and with a heavy heart,

151.play: Thinking on them, go I unto the Tower.

152.play: BUCKINGHAM: Think you, my lord, this little prating York

153.play: Was not incensed by his subtle mother

154.play: To taunt and scorn you thus opprobriously?

155.play: GLOUCESTER: No doubt, no doubt; O, 'tis a parlous boy;

157.play: He is all the mother's, from the top to toe.

158.play: BUCKINGHAM: Well, let them rest. Come hither, Catesby.

159.play: Thou art sworn as deeply to effect what we intend

160.play: As closely to conceal what we impart:

161.play: Thou know'st our reasons urged upon the way;

162.play: What think'st thou? is it not an easy matter

163.play: To make William Lord Hastings of our mind,

164.play: For the instalment of this noble duke

165.play: In the seat royal of this famous isle?

166.play: CATESBY: He for his father's sake so loves the prince,

167.play: That he will not be won to aught against him.

168.play: BUCKINGHAM: What think'st thou, then, of Stanley? what will he?

169.play: CATESBY: He will do all in all as Hastings doth.

170.play: BUCKINGHAM: Well, then, no more but this: go, gentle Catesby,

171.play: And, as it were far off sound thou Lord Hastings,

172.play: How doth he stand affected to our purpose;

173.play: And summon him to-morrow to the Tower,

175.play: If thou dost find him tractable to us,

176.play: Encourage him, and show him all our reasons:

177.play: If he be leaden, icy-cold, unwilling,

178.play: Be thou so too; and so break off your talk,

179.play: And give us notice of his inclination:

180.play: For we to-morrow hold divided councils,

181.play: Wherein thyself shalt highly be employ'd.

182.play: GLOUCESTER: Commend me to Lord William: tell him, Catesby,

183.play: His ancient knot of dangerous adversaries

184.play: To-morrow are let blood at Pomfret-castle;

185.play: And bid my friend, for joy of this good news,

186.play: Give mistress Shore one gentle kiss the more.

187.play: BUCKINGHAM: Good Catesby, go, effect this business soundly.

188.play: CATESBY: My good lords both, with all the heed I may.

189.play: GLOUCESTER: Shall we hear from you, Catesby, ere we sleep?

191.play: GLOUCESTER: At Crosby Place, there shall you find us both.

192.play: BUCKINGHAM: Now, my lord, what shall we do, if we perceive

193.play: Lord Hastings will not yield to our complots?

194.play: GLOUCESTER: Chop off his head, man; somewhat we will do:

195.play: And, look, when I am king, claim thou of me

196.play: The earldom of Hereford, and the moveables

197.play: Whereof the king my brother stood possess'd.

198.play: BUCKINGHAM: I'll claim that promise at your grace's hands.

199.play: GLOUCESTER: And look to have it yielded with all willingness.

200.play: Come, let us sup betimes, that afterwards

201.play: We may digest our complots in some form.

1.play: Enter a Messenger

2.play: HASTINGS: [Within]  Who knocks at the door?

3.play: Enter HASTINGS

4.play: HASTINGS: What is't o'clock?

5.play: Cannot thy master sleep these tedious nights?

6.play: And then?

7.play: Go, fellow, go, return unto thy lord;

8.play: Bid him not fear the separated councils

9.play: His honour and myself are at the one,

10.play: And at the other is my servant Catesby

11.play: Where nothing can proceed that toucheth us

12.play: Whereof I shall not have intelligence.

13.play: Tell him his fears are shallow, wanting instance:

14.play: And for his dreams, I wonder he is so fond

15.play: To trust the mockery of unquiet slumbers

16.play: To fly the boar before the boar pursues,

17.play: Were to incense the boar to follow us

18.play: And make pursuit where he did mean no chase.

19.play: Go, bid thy master rise and come to me

20.play: And we will both together to the Tower,

21.play: Where, he shall see, the boar will use us kindly.

22.play: Exit

23.play: CATESBY: Many good morrows to my noble lord!

24.play: HASTINGS: Good morrow, Catesby; you are early stirring

25.play: What news, what news, in this our tottering state?

26.play: CATESBY: It is a reeling world, indeed, my lord;

27.play: And I believe twill never stand upright

28.play: Till Richard wear the garland of the realm.

29.play: HASTINGS: How! wear the garland! dost thou mean the crown?

31.play: HASTINGS: I'll have this crown of mine cut from my shoulders

32.play: Ere I will see the crown so foul misplaced.

33.play: But canst thou guess that he doth aim at it?

34.play: CATESBY: Ay, on my life; and hopes to find forward

35.play: Upon his party for the gain thereof:

36.play: And thereupon he sends you this good news,

37.play: That this same very day your enemies,

38.play: The kindred of the queen, must die at Pomfret.

39.play: HASTINGS: Indeed, I am no mourner for that news,

40.play: Because they have been still mine enemies:

41.play: But, that I'll give my voice on Richard's side,

42.play: To bar my master's heirs in true descent,

43.play: God knows I will not do it, to the death.

44.play: CATESBY: God keep your lordship in that gracious mind!

45.play: HASTINGS: But I shall laugh at this a twelve-month hence,

46.play: That they who brought me in my master's hate

47.play: I live to look upon their tragedy.

50.play: HASTINGS: Ere a fortnight make me elder,

51.play: I'll send some packing that yet think not on it.

52.play: CATESBY: 'Tis a vile thing to die, my gracious lord,

53.play: When men are unprepared and look not for it.

54.play: HASTINGS: O monstrous, monstrous! and so falls it out

55.play: With Rivers, Vaughan, Grey: and so 'twill do

56.play: With some men else, who think themselves as safe

57.play: As thou and I; who, as thou know'st, are dear

58.play: To princely Richard and to Buckingham.

59.play: CATESBY: The princes both make high account of you;

60.play: For they account his head upon the bridge.

61.play: HASTINGS: I know they do; and I have well deserved it.

62.play: Come on, come on; where is your boar-spear, man?

63.play: Fear you the boar, and go so unprovided?

64.play: STANLEY: My lord, good morrow; good morrow, Catesby:

65.play: You may jest on, but, by the holy rood,

66.play: I do not like these several councils, I.

68.play: I hold my life as dear as you do yours;

69.play: And never in my life, I do protest,

70.play: Was it more precious to me than 'tis now:

71.play: Think you, but that I know our state secure,

72.play: I would be so triumphant as I am?

73.play: STANLEY: The lords at Pomfret, when they rode from London,

74.play: Were jocund, and supposed their state was sure,

75.play: And they indeed had no cause to mistrust;

76.play: But yet, you see how soon the day o'ercast.

77.play: This sudden stag of rancour I misdoubt:

78.play: Pray God, I say, I prove a needless coward!

79.play: What, shall we toward the Tower? the day is spent.

80.play: HASTINGS: Come, come, have with you. Wot you what, my lord?

81.play: To-day the lords you talk of are beheaded.

82.play: LORD STANLEY: They, for their truth, might better wear their heads

83.play: Than some that have accused them wear their hats.

84.play: But come, my lord, let us away.

85.play: HASTINGS: Go on before; I'll talk with this good fellow.

86.play: How now, sirrah! how goes the world with thee?

87.play: I tell thee, man, 'tis better with me now

88.play: Than when I met thee last where now we meet:

89.play: Then was I going prisoner to the Tower,

90.play: By the suggestion of the queen's allies;

91.play: But now, I tell thee--keep it to thyself--

92.play: This day those enemies are put to death,

93.play: And I in better state than e'er I was.

94.play: Gramercy, fellow: there, drink that for me.

96.play: HASTINGS: I thank thee, good Sir John, with all my heart.

97.play: I am in your debt for your last exercise;

98.play: Come the next Sabbath, and I will content you.

99.play: BUCKINGHAM: What, talking with a priest, lord chamberlain?

100.play: Your friends at Pomfret, they do need the priest;

101.play: Your honour hath no shriving work in hand.

102.play: HASTINGS: Good faith, and when I met this holy man,

103.play: Those men you talk of came into my mind.

104.play: What, go you toward the Tower?

105.play: BUCKINGHAM: I do, my lord; but long I shall not stay

106.play: I shall return before your lordship thence.

107.play: HASTINGS: 'Tis like enough, for I stay dinner there.

108.play: BUCKINGHAM: [Aside]  And supper too, although thou know'st it not.

110.play: HASTINGS: I'll wait upon your lordship.

1.play: Enter RATCLIFF, with halberds, carrying RIVERS, GREY, and VAUGHAN to death

2.play: RATCLIFF: Come, bring forth the prisoners.

3.play: RIVERS: Sir Richard Ratcliff, let me tell thee this:

4.play: To-day shalt thou behold a subject die

5.play: For truth, for duty, and for loyalty.

6.play: GREY: God keep the prince from all the pack of you!

7.play: A knot you are of damned blood-suckers!

8.play: VAUGHAN: You live that shall cry woe for this after.

9.play: RATCLIFF: Dispatch; the limit of your lives is out.

10.play: RIVERS: O Pomfret, Pomfret! O thou bloody prison,

11.play: Fatal and ominous to noble peers!

12.play: Within the guilty closure of thy walls

13.play: Richard the second here was hack'd to death;

14.play: And, for more slander to thy dismal seat,

15.play: We give thee up our guiltless blood to drink.

16.play: GREY: Now Margaret's curse is fall'n upon our heads,

17.play: For standing by when Richard stabb'd her son.

18.play: RIVERS: Then cursed she Hastings, then cursed she Buckingham,

19.play: Then cursed she Richard. O, remember, God

20.play: To hear her prayers for them, as now for us

21.play: And for my sister and her princely sons,

22.play: Be satisfied, dear God, with our true blood,

23.play: Which, as thou know'st, unjustly must be spilt.

24.play: RATCLIFF: Make haste; the hour of death is expiate.

25.play: RIVERS: Come, Grey, come, Vaughan, let us all embrace:

26.play: And take our leave, until we meet in heaven.

1.play: Enter BUCKINGHAM, DERBY, HASTINGS, the BISHOP OF  ELY, RATCLIFF, LOVEL, with others, and take their seats at a table

2.play: HASTINGS: My lords, at once: the cause why we are met

3.play: Is, to determine of the coronation.

4.play: In God's name, speak: when is the royal day?

5.play: BUCKINGHAM: Are all things fitting for that royal time?

6.play: DERBY: It is, and wants but nomination.

7.play: BISHOP OF ELY: To-morrow, then, I judge a happy day.

8.play: BUCKINGHAM: Who knows the lord protector's mind herein?

9.play: Who is most inward with the royal duke?

10.play: BISHOP OF ELY: Your grace, we think, should soonest know his mind.

11.play: BUCKINGHAM: Who, I, my lord I we know each other's faces,

12.play: But for our hearts, he knows no more of mine,

13.play: Than I of yours;

14.play: Nor I no more of his, than you of mine.

15.play: Lord Hastings, you and he are near in love.

16.play: HASTINGS: I thank his grace, I know he loves me well;

17.play: But, for his purpose in the coronation.

18.play: I have not sounded him, nor he deliver'd

19.play: His gracious pleasure any way therein:

20.play: But you, my noble lords, may name the time;

21.play: And in the duke's behalf I'll give my voice,

22.play: Which, I presume, he'll take in gentle part.

23.play: BISHOP OF ELY: Now in good time, here comes the duke himself.

24.play: GLOUCESTER: My noble lords and cousins all, good morrow.

25.play: I have been long a sleeper; but, I hope,

26.play: My absence doth neglect no great designs,

27.play: Which by my presence might have been concluded.

28.play: BUCKINGHAM: Had not you come upon your cue, my lord

29.play: William Lord Hastings had pronounced your part,--

30.play: I mean, your voice,--for crowning of the king.

31.play: GLOUCESTER: Than my Lord Hastings no man might be bolder;

32.play: His lordship knows me well, and loves me well.

36.play: GLOUCESTER: When I was last in Holborn,

37.play: I saw good strawberries in your garden there

38.play: I do beseech you send for some of them.

39.play: BISHOP OF ELY: Marry, and will, my lord, with all my heart.

40.play: GLOUCESTER: Cousin of Buckingham, a word with you.

41.play: Catesby hath sounded Hastings in our business,

42.play: And finds the testy gentleman so hot,

43.play: As he will lose his head ere give consent

44.play: His master's son, as worshipful as he terms it,

45.play: Shall lose the royalty of England's throne.

46.play: BUCKINGHAM: Withdraw you hence, my lord, I'll follow you.

47.play: DERBY: We have not yet set down this day of triumph.

48.play: To-morrow, in mine opinion, is too sudden;

49.play: For I myself am not so well provided

50.play: As else I would be, were the day prolong'd.

51.play: BISHOP OF ELY: Where is my lord protector? I have sent for these

53.play: HASTINGS: His grace looks cheerfully and smooth to-day;

54.play: There's some conceit or other likes him well,

55.play: When he doth bid good morrow with such a spirit.

56.play: I think there's never a man in Christendom

57.play: That can less hide his love or hate than he;

58.play: For by his face straight shall you know his heart.

59.play: DERBY: What of his heart perceive you in his face

60.play: By any likelihood he show'd to-day?

61.play: HASTINGS: Marry, that with no man here he is offended;

62.play: For, were he, he had shown it in his looks.

63.play: DERBY: I pray God he be not, I say.

64.play: GLOUCESTER: I pray you all, tell me what they deserve

65.play: That do conspire my death with devilish plots

66.play: Of damned witchcraft, and that have prevail'd

67.play: Upon my body with their hellish charms?

68.play: HASTINGS: The tender love I bear your grace, my lord,

69.play: Makes me most forward in this noble presence

70.play: To doom the offenders, whatsoever they be

71.play: I say, my lord, they have deserved death.

72.play: GLOUCESTER: Then be your eyes the witness of this ill:

73.play: See how I am bewitch'd; behold mine arm

74.play: Is, like a blasted sapling, wither'd up:

75.play: And this is Edward's wife, that monstrous witch,

76.play: Consorted with that harlot strumpet Shore,

77.play: That by their witchcraft thus have marked me.

78.play: HASTINGS: If they have done this thing, my gracious lord--

79.play: GLOUCESTER: If I thou protector of this damned strumpet--

80.play: Tellest thou me of 'ifs'?  Thou art a traitor:

81.play: Off with his head! Now, by Saint Paul I swear,

82.play: I will not dine until I see the same.

83.play: Lovel and Ratcliff, look that it be done:

84.play: The rest, that love me, rise and follow me.

85.play: HASTINGS: Woe, woe for England! not a whit for me;

86.play: For I, too fond, might have prevented this.

87.play: Stanley did dream the boar did raze his helm;

88.play: But I disdain'd it, and did scorn to fly:

89.play: Three times to-day my foot-cloth horse did stumble,

90.play: And startled, when he look'd upon the Tower,

91.play: As loath to bear me to the slaughter-house.

92.play: O, now I want the priest that spake to me:

93.play: I now repent I told the pursuivant

95.play: How they at Pomfret bloodily were butcher'd,

96.play: And I myself secure in grace and favour.

97.play: O Margaret, Margaret, now thy heavy curse

98.play: Is lighted on poor Hastings' wretched head!

99.play: RATCLIFF: Dispatch, my lord; the duke would be at dinner:

100.play: Make a short shrift; he longs to see your head.

101.play: HASTINGS: O momentary grace of mortal men,

102.play: Which we more hunt for than the grace of God!

103.play: Who builds his hopes in air of your good looks,

104.play: Lives like a drunken sailor on a mast,

105.play: Ready, with every nod, to tumble down

106.play: Into the fatal bowels of the deep.

107.play: LOVEL: Come, come, dispatch; 'tis bootless to exclaim.

108.play: HASTINGS: O bloody Richard! miserable England!

109.play: I prophesy the fearful'st time to thee

110.play: That ever wretched age hath look'd upon.

111.play: Come, lead me to the block; bear him my head.

112.play: They smile at me that shortly shall be dead.

1.play: Enter GLOUCESTER and BUCKINGHAM, in rotten armour, marvellous ill-favoured

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Come, cousin, canst thou quake, and change thy colour,

3.play: Murder thy breath in the middle of a word,

4.play: And then begin again, and stop again,

5.play: As if thou wert distraught and mad with terror?

6.play: BUCKINGHAM: Tut, I can counterfeit the deep tragedian;

7.play: Speak and look back, and pry on every side,

8.play: Tremble and start at wagging of a straw,

9.play: Intending deep suspicion: ghastly looks

10.play: Are at my service, like enforced smiles;

11.play: And both are ready in their offices,

12.play: At any time, to grace my stratagems.

13.play: But what, is Catesby gone?

14.play: GLOUCESTER: He is; and, see, he brings the mayor along.

15.play: BUCKINGHAM: Lord mayor,--

16.play: GLOUCESTER: Look to the drawbridge there!

17.play: BUCKINGHAM: Hark! a drum.

18.play: GLOUCESTER: Catesby, o'erlook the walls.

19.play: BUCKINGHAM: Lord mayor, the reason we have sent--

20.play: GLOUCESTER: Look back, defend thee, here are enemies.

21.play: BUCKINGHAM: God and our innocency defend and guard us!

22.play: GLOUCESTER: Be patient, they are friends, Ratcliff and Lovel.

23.play: LOVEL: Here is the head of that ignoble traitor,

24.play: The dangerous and unsuspected Hastings.

25.play: GLOUCESTER: So dear I loved the man, that I must weep.

26.play: I took him for the plainest harmless creature

27.play: That breathed upon this earth a Christian;

28.play: Made him my book wherein my soul recorded

29.play: The history of all her secret thoughts:

30.play: So smooth he daub'd his vice with show of virtue,

31.play: That, his apparent open guilt omitted,

32.play: I mean, his conversation with Shore's wife,

33.play: He lived from all attainder of suspect.

34.play: BUCKINGHAM: Well, well, he was the covert'st shelter'd traitor

36.play: Would you imagine, or almost believe,

37.play: Were't not that, by great preservation,

38.play: We live to tell it you, the subtle traitor

39.play: This day had plotted, in the council-house

40.play: To murder me and my good Lord of Gloucester?

41.play: GLOUCESTER: What, think You we are Turks or infidels?

42.play: Or that we would, against the form of law,

43.play: Proceed thus rashly to the villain's death,

44.play: But that the extreme peril of the case,

45.play: The peace of England and our persons' safety,

47.play: Yet had not we determined he should die,

48.play: Until your lordship came to see his death;

49.play: Which now the loving haste of these our friends,

50.play: Somewhat against our meaning, have prevented:

51.play: Because, my lord, we would have had you heard

52.play: The traitor speak, and timorously confess

53.play: The manner and the purpose of his treason;

54.play: That you might well have signified the same

55.play: Unto the citizens, who haply may

56.play: Misconstrue us in him and wail his death.

57.play: And to that end we wish'd your lord-ship here,

58.play: To avoid the carping censures of the world.

59.play: BUCKINGHAM: But since you come too late of our intents,

60.play: Yet witness what you hear we did intend:

61.play: And so, my good lord mayor, we bid farewell.

62.play: GLOUCESTER: Go, after, after, cousin Buckingham.

63.play: The mayor towards Guildhall hies him in all post:

64.play: There, at your meet'st advantage of the time,

65.play: Infer the bastardy of Edward's children:

66.play: Tell them how Edward put to death a citizen,

67.play: Only for saying he would make his son

68.play: Heir to the crown; meaning indeed his house,

69.play: Which, by the sign thereof was termed so.

71.play: And bestial appetite in change of lust;

72.play: Which stretched to their servants, daughters, wives,

73.play: Even where his lustful eye or savage heart,

74.play: Without control, listed to make his prey.

75.play: Nay, for a need, thus far come near my person:

76.play: Tell them, when that my mother went with child

77.play: Of that unsatiate Edward, noble York

78.play: My princely father then had wars in France

79.play: And, by just computation of the time,

80.play: Found that the issue was not his begot;

81.play: Which well appeared in his lineaments,

82.play: Being nothing like the noble duke my father:

83.play: But touch this sparingly, as 'twere far off,

84.play: Because you know, my lord, my mother lives.

85.play: BUCKINGHAM: Fear not, my lord, I'll play the orator

86.play: As if the golden fee for which I plead

87.play: Were for myself: and so, my lord, adieu.

88.play: GLOUCESTER: If you thrive well, bring them to Baynard's Castle;

89.play: Where you shall find me well accompanied

90.play: With reverend fathers and well-learned bishops.

91.play: BUCKINGHAM: I go: and towards three or four o'clock

92.play: Look for the news that the Guildhall affords.

93.play: GLOUCESTER: Go, Lovel, with all speed to Doctor Shaw;

94.play: Go thou to Friar Penker; bid them both

95.play: Meet me within this hour at Baynard's Castle.

96.play: Now will I in, to take some privy order,

97.play: To draw the brats of Clarence out of sight;

98.play: And to give notice, that no manner of person

99.play: At any time have recourse unto the princes.

1.play: Enter GLOUCESTER and BUCKINGHAM, at several doors

2.play: GLOUCESTER: How now, my lord, what say the citizens?

3.play: BUCKINGHAM: Now, by the holy mother of our Lord,

4.play: The citizens are mum and speak not a word.

5.play: GLOUCESTER: Touch'd you the bastardy of Edward's children?

6.play: BUCKINGHAM: I did; with his contract with Lady Lucy,

7.play: And his contract by deputy in France;

8.play: The insatiate greediness of his desires,

9.play: And his enforcement of the city wives;

10.play: His tyranny for trifles; his own bastardy,

11.play: As being got, your father then in France,

12.play: His resemblance, being not like the duke;

13.play: Withal I did infer your lineaments,

14.play: Being the right idea of your father,

15.play: Both in your form and nobleness of mind;

16.play: Laid open all your victories in Scotland,

17.play: Your dicipline in war, wisdom in peace,

18.play: Your bounty, virtue, fair humility:

19.play: Indeed, left nothing fitting for the purpose

20.play: Untouch'd, or slightly handled, in discourse

21.play: And when mine oratory grew to an end

22.play: I bid them that did love their country's good

23.play: Cry 'God save Richard, England's royal king!'

24.play: GLOUCESTER: Ah! and did they so?

25.play: BUCKINGHAM: No, so God help me, they spake not a word;

26.play: But, like dumb statues or breathing stones,

27.play: Gazed each on other, and look'd deadly pale.

28.play: Which when I saw, I reprehended them;

29.play: And ask'd the mayor what meant this wilful silence:

30.play: His answer was, the people were not wont

31.play: To be spoke to but by the recorder.

32.play: Then he was urged to tell my tale again,

33.play: 'Thus saith the duke, thus hath the duke inferr'd;'

34.play: But nothing spake in warrant from himself.

35.play: When he had done, some followers of mine own,

36.play: At the lower end of the hall, hurl'd up their caps,

37.play: And some ten voices cried 'God save King Richard!'

38.play: And thus I took the vantage of those few,

39.play: 'Thanks, gentle citizens and friends,' quoth I;

41.play: Argues your wisdoms and your love to Richard:'

42.play: And even here brake off, and came away.

43.play: GLOUCESTER: What tongueless blocks were they! would not they speak?

44.play: BUCKINGHAM: No, by my troth, my lord.

45.play: GLOUCESTER: Will not the mayor then and his brethren come?

46.play: BUCKINGHAM: The mayor is here at hand: intend some fear;

47.play: Be not you spoke with, but by mighty suit:

48.play: And look you get a prayer-book in your hand,

49.play: And stand betwixt two churchmen, good my lord;

50.play: For on that ground I'll build a holy descant:

51.play: And be not easily won to our request:

52.play: Play the maid's part, still answer nay, and take it.

53.play: GLOUCESTER: I go; and if you plead as well for them

54.play: As I can say nay to thee for myself,

55.play: No doubt well bring it to a happy issue.

56.play: BUCKINGHAM: Go, go, up to the leads; the lord mayor knocks.

57.play: Welcome my lord; I dance attendance here;

58.play: I think the duke will not be spoke withal.

59.play: Here comes his servant: how now, Catesby,

61.play: CATESBY: My lord: he doth entreat your grace;

62.play: To visit him to-morrow or next day:

63.play: He is within, with two right reverend fathers,

65.play: And no worldly suit would he be moved,

66.play: To draw him from his holy exercise.

67.play: BUCKINGHAM: Return, good Catesby, to thy lord again;

68.play: Tell him, myself, the mayor and citizens,

69.play: In deep designs and matters of great moment,

70.play: No less importing than our general good,

71.play: Are come to have some conference with his grace.

72.play: CATESBY: I'll tell him what you say, my lord.

73.play: BUCKINGHAM: Ah, ha, my lord, this prince is not an Edward!

74.play: He is not lolling on a lewd day-bed,

75.play: But on his knees at meditation;

76.play: Not dallying with a brace of courtezans,

77.play: But meditating with two deep divines;

78.play: Not sleeping, to engross his idle body,

79.play: But praying, to enrich his watchful soul:

80.play: Happy were England, would this gracious prince

81.play: Take on himself the sovereignty thereof:

82.play: But, sure, I fear, we shall ne'er win him to it.

84.play: How now, Catesby, what says your lord?

86.play: He wonders to what end you have assembled

87.play: Such troops of citizens to speak with him,

88.play: His grace not being warn'd thereof before:

89.play: My lord, he fears you mean no good to him.

90.play: BUCKINGHAM: Sorry I am my noble cousin should

91.play: Suspect me, that I mean no good to him:

92.play: By heaven, I come in perfect love to him;

93.play: And so once more return and tell his grace.

94.play: When holy and devout religious men

95.play: Are at their beads, 'tis hard to draw them thence,

97.play: Two props of virtue for a Christian prince,

98.play: To stay him from the fall of vanity:

99.play: And, see, a book of prayer in his hand,

100.play: True ornaments to know a holy man.

102.play: Lend favourable ears to our request;

104.play: Of thy devotion and right Christian zeal.

105.play: GLOUCESTER: My lord, there needs no such apology:

106.play: I rather do beseech you pardon me,

107.play: Who, earnest in the service of my God,

108.play: Neglect the visitation of my friends.

109.play: But, leaving this, what is your grace's pleasure?

110.play: BUCKINGHAM: Even that, I hope, which pleaseth God above,

111.play: And all good men of this ungovern'd isle.

112.play: GLOUCESTER: I do suspect I have done some offence

113.play: That seems disgracious in the city's eyes,

114.play: And that you come to reprehend my ignorance.

115.play: BUCKINGHAM: You have, my lord: would it might please your grace,

116.play: At our entreaties, to amend that fault!

117.play: GLOUCESTER: Else wherefore breathe I in a Christian land?

118.play: BUCKINGHAM: Then know, it is your fault that you resign

119.play: The supreme seat, the throne majestical,

120.play: The scepter'd office of your ancestors,

121.play: Your state of fortune and your due of birth,

122.play: The lineal glory of your royal house,

123.play: To the corruption of a blemished stock:

124.play: Whilst, in the mildness of your sleepy thoughts,

125.play: Which here we waken to our country's good,

126.play: This noble isle doth want her proper limbs;

127.play: Her face defaced with scars of infamy,

128.play: Her royal stock graft with ignoble plants,

129.play: And almost shoulder'd in the swallowing gulf

130.play: Of blind forgetfulness and dark oblivion.

131.play: Which to recure, we heartily solicit

132.play: Your gracious self to take on you the charge

133.play: And kingly government of this your land,

135.play: Or lowly factor for another's gain;

136.play: But as successively from blood to blood,

137.play: Your right of birth, your empery, your own.

138.play: For this, consorted with the citizens,

139.play: Your very worshipful and loving friends,

141.play: In this just suit come I to move your grace.

142.play: GLOUCESTER: I know not whether to depart in silence,

143.play: Or bitterly to speak in your reproof.

144.play: Best fitteth my degree or your condition

145.play: If not to answer, you might haply think

147.play: To bear the golden yoke of sovereignty,

148.play: Which fondly you would here impose on me;

149.play: If to reprove you for this suit of yours,

150.play: So season'd with your faithful love to me.

151.play: Then, on the other side, I cheque'd my friends.

152.play: Therefore, to speak, and to avoid the first,

153.play: And then, in speaking, not to incur the last,

155.play: Your love deserves my thanks; but my desert

157.play: First if all obstacles were cut away,

158.play: And that my path were even to the crown,

159.play: As my ripe revenue and due by birth

160.play: Yet so much is my poverty of spirit,

161.play: So mighty and so many my defects,

162.play: As I had rather hide me from my greatness,

163.play: Being a bark to brook no mighty sea,

164.play: Than in my greatness covet to be hid,

165.play: And in the vapour of my glory smother'd.

166.play: But, God be thank'd, there's no need of me,

167.play: And much I need to help you, if need were;

168.play: The royal tree hath left us royal fruit,

169.play: Which, mellow'd by the stealing hours of time,

170.play: Will well become the seat of majesty,

171.play: And make, no doubt, us happy by his reign.

172.play: On him I lay what you would lay on me,

173.play: The right and fortune of his happy stars;

174.play: Which God defend that I should wring from him!

175.play: BUCKINGHAM: My lord, this argues conscience in your grace;

176.play: But the respects thereof are nice and trivial,

178.play: You say that Edward is your brother's son:

179.play: So say we too, but not by Edward's wife;

180.play: For first he was contract to Lady Lucy--

181.play: Your mother lives a witness to that vow--

183.play: To Bona, sister to the King of France.

184.play: These both put by a poor petitioner,

185.play: A care-crazed mother of a many children,

187.play: Even in the afternoon of her best days,

188.play: Made prize and purchase of his lustful eye,

189.play: Seduced the pitch and height of all his thoughts

190.play: To base declension and loathed bigamy

191.play: By her, in his unlawful bed, he got

192.play: This Edward, whom our manners term the prince.

194.play: Save that, for reverence to some alive,

195.play: I give a sparing limit to my tongue.

196.play: Then, good my lord, take to your royal self

198.play: If non to bless us and the land withal,

199.play: Yet to draw forth your noble ancestry

200.play: From the corruption of abusing times,

202.play: Refuse not, mighty lord, this proffer'd love.

203.play: CATESBY: O, make them joyful, grant their lawful suit!

204.play: GLOUCESTER: Alas, why would you heap these cares on me?

205.play: I am unfit for state and majesty;

206.play: I do beseech you, take it not amiss;

207.play: I cannot nor I will not yield to you.

208.play: BUCKINGHAM: If you refuse it,--as, in love and zeal,

209.play: Loath to depose the child, Your brother's son;

210.play: As well we know your tenderness of heart

212.play: Which we have noted in you to your kin,

213.play: And egally indeed to all estates,--

214.play: Yet whether you accept our suit or no,

215.play: Your brother's son shall never reign our king;

216.play: But we will plant some other in the throne,

217.play: To the disgrace and downfall of your house:

218.play: And in this resolution here we leave you.--

219.play: Come, citizens: 'zounds! I'll entreat no more.

220.play: GLOUCESTER: O, do not swear, my lord of Buckingham.

221.play: CATESBY: Call them again, my lord, and accept their suit.

222.play: ANOTHER: Do, good my lord, lest all the land do rue it.

223.play: GLOUCESTER: Would you enforce me to a world of care?

224.play: Well, call them again. I am not made of stone,

225.play: But penetrable to your. kind entreats,

226.play: Albeit against my conscience and my soul.

227.play: Cousin of Buckingham, and you sage, grave men,

228.play: Since you will buckle fortune on my back,

229.play: To bear her burthen, whether I will or no,

230.play: I must have patience to endure the load:

231.play: But if black scandal or foul-faced reproach

232.play: Attend the sequel of your imposition,

233.play: Your mere enforcement shall acquittance me

234.play: From all the impure blots and stains thereof;

235.play: For God he knows, and you may partly see,

236.play: How far I am from the desire thereof.

237.play: In saying so, you shall but say the truth.

238.play: BUCKINGHAM: Then I salute you with this kingly title:

239.play: Long live Richard, England's royal king!

240.play: To-morrow will it please you to be crown'd?

241.play: GLOUCESTER: Even when you please, since you will have it so.

242.play: BUCKINGHAM: To-morrow, then, we will attend your grace:

243.play: And so most joyfully we take our leave.

244.play: GLOUCESTER: Come, let us to our holy task again.

245.play: Farewell, good cousin; farewell, gentle friends.

3.play: Led in the hand of her kind aunt of Gloucester?

4.play: Now, for my life, she's wandering to the Tower,

6.play: Daughter, well met.

42.play: Death and destruction dog thee at the heels;

45.play: And live with Richmond, from the reach of hell

53.play: To meet you on the way, and welcome you.

57.play: A cockatrice hast thou hatch'd to the world,

59.play: LORD STANLEY: Come, madam, come; I in all haste was sent.

61.play: I would to God that the inclusive verge

65.play: And die, ere men can say, God save the queen!

15.play: A book of prayers on their pillow lay;

16.play: Which once,' quoth Forrest, 'almost changed my mind;

17.play: But O! the devil'--there the villain stopp'd

18.play: Whilst Dighton thus told on: 'We smothered

19.play: The most replenished sweet work of nature,

20.play: That from the prime creation e'er she framed.'

21.play: Thus both are gone with conscience and remorse;

22.play: They could not speak; and so I left them both,

23.play: To bring this tidings to the bloody king.

24.play: And here he comes.

25.play: All hail, my sovereign liege!

26.play: KING RICHARD III: Kind Tyrrel, am I happy in thy news?

27.play: TYRREL: If to have done the thing you gave in charge

28.play: Beget your happiness, be happy then,

29.play: For it is done, my lord.

30.play: KING RICHARD III: But didst thou see them dead?

32.play: KING RICHARD III: And buried, gentle Tyrrel?

33.play: TYRREL: The chaplain of the Tower hath buried them;

34.play: But how or in what place I do not know.

35.play: KING RICHARD III: Come to me, Tyrrel, soon at after supper,

36.play: And thou shalt tell the process of their death.

37.play: Meantime, but think how I may do thee good,

38.play: And be inheritor of thy desire.

40.play: The son of Clarence have I pent up close;

41.play: His daughter meanly have I match'd in marriage;

42.play: The sons of Edward sleep in Abraham's bosom,

43.play: And Anne my wife hath bid the world good night.

44.play: Now, for I know the Breton Richmond aims

45.play: At young Elizabeth, my brother's daughter,

46.play: And, by that knot, looks proudly o'er the crown,

47.play: To her I go, a jolly thriving wooer.

49.play: KING RICHARD III: Good news or bad, that thou comest in so bluntly?

50.play: CATESBY: Bad news, my lord: Ely is fled to Richmond;

51.play: And Buckingham, back'd with the hardy Welshmen,

52.play: Is in the field, and still his power increaseth.

53.play: KING RICHARD III: Ely with Richmond troubles me more near

54.play: Than Buckingham and his rash-levied army.

55.play: Come, I have heard that fearful commenting

56.play: Is leaden servitor to dull delay;

57.play: Delay leads impotent and snail-paced beggary

58.play: Then fiery expedition be my wing,

59.play: Jove's Mercury, and herald for a king!

61.play: We must be brief when traitors brave the field.

1.play: Enter QUEEN MARGARET

2.play: QUEEN MARGARET: So, now prosperity begins to mellow

3.play: And drop into the rotten mouth of death.

4.play: Here in these confines slily have I lurk'd,

5.play: To watch the waning of mine adversaries.

6.play: A dire induction am I witness to,

7.play: And will to France, hoping the consequence

8.play: Will prove as bitter, black, and tragical.

9.play: Withdraw thee, wretched Margaret: who comes here?

10.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Ah, my young princes! ah, my tender babes!

11.play: My unblown flowers, new-appearing sweets!

12.play: If yet your gentle souls fly in the air

13.play: And be not fix'd in doom perpetual,

14.play: Hover about me with your airy wings

15.play: And hear your mother's lamentation!

16.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Hover about her; say, that right for right

17.play: Hath dimm'd your infant morn to aged night.

18.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: So many miseries have crazed my voice,

20.play: Edward Plantagenet, why art thou dead?

21.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Plantagenet doth quit Plantagenet.

22.play: Edward for Edward pays a dying debt.

23.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Wilt thou, O God, fly from such gentle lambs,

25.play: When didst thou sleep when such a deed was done?

26.play: QUEEN MARGARET: When holy Harry died, and my sweet son.

28.play: Woe's scene, world's shame, grave's due by life usurp'd,

29.play: Brief abstract and record of tedious days,

30.play: Rest thy unrest on England's lawful earth,

31.play: Unlawfully made drunk with innocents' blood!

32.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O, that thou wouldst as well afford a grave

34.play: Then would I hide my bones, not rest them here.

35.play: O, who hath any cause to mourn but I?

36.play: QUEEN MARGARET: If ancient sorrow be most reverend,

37.play: Give mine the benefit of seniory,

38.play: And let my woes frown on the upper hand.

40.play: Tell o'er your woes again by viewing mine:

41.play: I had an Edward, till a Richard kill'd him;

42.play: I had a Harry, till a Richard kill'd him:

43.play: Thou hadst an Edward, till a Richard kill'd him;

44.play: Thou hadst a Richard, till a Richard killed him;

45.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I had a Richard too, and thou didst kill him;

46.play: I had a Rutland too, thou holp'st to kill him.

47.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Thou hadst a Clarence too, and Richard kill'd him.

48.play: From forth the kennel of thy womb hath crept

49.play: A hell-hound that doth hunt us all to death:

50.play: That dog, that had his teeth before his eyes,

51.play: To worry lambs and lap their gentle blood,

52.play: That foul defacer of God's handiwork,

53.play: That excellent grand tyrant of the earth,

54.play: That reigns in galled eyes of weeping souls,

55.play: Thy womb let loose, to chase us to our graves.

56.play: O upright, just, and true-disposing God,

57.play: How do I thank thee, that this carnal cur

58.play: Preys on the issue of his mother's body,

59.play: And makes her pew-fellow with others' moan!

60.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: O Harry's wife, triumph not in my woes!

61.play: God witness with me, I have wept for thine.

62.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Bear with me; I am hungry for revenge,

63.play: And now I cloy me with beholding it.

64.play: Thy Edward he is dead, that stabb'd my Edward:

65.play: Thy other Edward dead, to quit my Edward;

66.play: Young York he is but boot, because both they

67.play: Match not the high perfection of my loss:

68.play: Thy Clarence he is dead that kill'd my Edward;

69.play: And the beholders of this tragic play,

70.play: The adulterate Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, Grey,

71.play: Untimely smother'd in their dusky graves.

72.play: Richard yet lives, hell's black intelligencer,

73.play: Only reserved their factor, to buy souls

74.play: And send them thither: but at hand, at hand,

75.play: Ensues his piteous and unpitied end:

76.play: Earth gapes, hell burns, fiends roar, saints pray.

77.play: To have him suddenly convey'd away.

78.play: Cancel his bond of life, dear God, I prey,

79.play: That I may live to say, The dog is dead!

80.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O, thou didst prophesy the time would come

81.play: That I should wish for thee to help me curse

82.play: That bottled spider, that foul bunch-back'd toad!

83.play: QUEEN MARGARET: I call'd thee then vain flourish of my fortune;

84.play: I call'd thee then poor shadow, painted queen;

85.play: The presentation of but what I was;

86.play: The flattering index of a direful pageant;

87.play: One heaved a-high, to be hurl'd down below;

88.play: A mother only mock'd with two sweet babes;

89.play: A dream of what thou wert, a breath, a bubble,

90.play: A sign of dignity, a garish flag,

91.play: To be the aim of every dangerous shot,

92.play: A queen in jest, only to fill the scene.

93.play: Where is thy husband now? where be thy brothers?

94.play: Where are thy children? wherein dost thou, joy?

95.play: Who sues to thee and cries 'God save the queen'?

96.play: Where be the bending peers that flatter'd thee?

97.play: Where be the thronging troops that follow'd thee?

98.play: Decline all this, and see what now thou art:

99.play: For happy wife, a most distressed widow;

100.play: For joyful mother, one that wails the name;

101.play: For queen, a very caitiff crown'd with care;

102.play: For one being sued to, one that humbly sues;

103.play: For one that scorn'd at me, now scorn'd of me;

104.play: For one being fear'd of all, now fearing one;

105.play: For one commanding all, obey'd of none.

106.play: Thus hath the course of justice wheel'd about,

107.play: And left thee but a very prey to time;

108.play: Having no more but thought of what thou wert,

109.play: To torture thee the more, being what thou art.

110.play: Thou didst usurp my place, and dost thou not

111.play: Usurp the just proportion of my sorrow?

112.play: Now thy proud neck bears half my burthen'd yoke;

113.play: From which even here I slip my weary neck,

114.play: And leave the burthen of it all on thee.

115.play: Farewell, York's wife, and queen of sad mischance:

116.play: These English woes will make me smile in France.

117.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O thou well skill'd in curses, stay awhile,

118.play: And teach me how to curse mine enemies!

119.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Forbear to sleep the nights, and fast the days;

120.play: Compare dead happiness with living woe;

121.play: Think that thy babes were fairer than they were,

122.play: And he that slew them fouler than he is:

123.play: Bettering thy loss makes the bad causer worse:

124.play: Revolving this will teach thee how to curse.

125.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: My words are dull; O, quicken them with thine!

126.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Thy woes will make them sharp, and pierce like mine.

127.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Why should calamity be full of words?

128.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Windy attorneys to their client woes,

131.play: Let them have scope: though what they do impart

132.play: Help not all, yet do they ease the heart.

133.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: If so, then be not tongue-tied: go with me.

134.play: And in the breath of bitter words let's smother

135.play: My damned son, which thy two sweet sons smother'd.

136.play: I hear his drum: be copious in exclaims.

137.play: KING RICHARD III: Who intercepts my expedition?

138.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: O, she that might have intercepted thee,

139.play: By strangling thee in her accursed womb

140.play: From all the slaughters, wretch, that thou hast done!

141.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Hidest thou that forehead with a golden crown,

142.play: Where should be graven, if that right were right,

143.play: The slaughter of the prince that owed that crown,

144.play: And the dire death of my two sons and brothers?

145.play: Tell me, thou villain slave, where are my children?

146.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Thou toad, thou toad, where is thy brother Clarence?

147.play: And little Ned Plantagenet, his son?

148.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Where is kind Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, Grey?

149.play: KING RICHARD III: A flourish, trumpets! strike alarum, drums!

150.play: Let not the heavens hear these tell-tale women

151.play: Rail on the Lord's enointed: strike, I say!

152.play: Either be patient, and entreat me fair,

153.play: Or with the clamorous report of war

154.play: Thus will I drown your exclamations.

155.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Art thou my son?

156.play: KING RICHARD III: Ay, I thank God, my father, and yourself.

157.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Then patiently hear my impatience.

158.play: KING RICHARD III: Madam, I have a touch of your condition,

159.play: Which cannot brook the accent of reproof.

160.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: O, let me speak!

161.play: KING RICHARD III: Do then: but I'll not hear.

162.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I will be mild and gentle in my speech.

163.play: KING RICHARD III: And brief, good mother; for I am in haste.

164.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Art thou so hasty? I have stay'd for thee,

165.play: God knows, in anguish, pain and agony.

166.play: KING RICHARD III: And came I not at last to comfort you?

167.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: No, by the holy rood, thou know'st it well,

168.play: Thou camest on earth to make the earth my hell.

169.play: A grievous burthen was thy birth to me;

170.play: Tetchy and wayward was thy infancy;

171.play: Thy school-days frightful, desperate, wild, and furious,

172.play: Thy prime of manhood daring, bold, and venturous,

173.play: Thy age confirm'd, proud, subdued, bloody,

175.play: More mild, but yet more harmful, kind in hatred:

176.play: What comfortable hour canst thou name,

177.play: That ever graced me in thy company?

178.play: KING RICHARD III: Faith, none, but Humphrey Hour, that call'd

180.play: To breakfast once forth of my company.

181.play: If I be so disgracious in your sight,

182.play: Let me march on, and not offend your grace.

184.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I prithee, hear me speak.

185.play: KING RICHARD III: You speak too bitterly.

186.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Hear me a word;

187.play: For I shall never speak to thee again.

189.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Either thou wilt die, by God's just ordinance,

190.play: Ere from this war thou turn a conqueror,

191.play: Or I with grief and extreme age shall perish

192.play: And never look upon thy face again.

193.play: Therefore take with thee my most heavy curse;

194.play: Which, in the day of battle, tire thee more

195.play: Than all the complete armour that thou wear'st!

196.play: My prayers on the adverse party fight;

197.play: And there the little souls of Edward's children

198.play: Whisper the spirits of thine enemies

199.play: And promise them success and victory.

200.play: Bloody thou art, bloody will be thy end;

201.play: Shame serves thy life and doth thy death attend.

202.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Though far more cause, yet much less spirit to curse

203.play: Abides in me; I say amen to all.

204.play: KING RICHARD III: Stay, madam; I must speak a word with you.

205.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: I have no more sons of the royal blood

206.play: For thee to murder: for my daughters, Richard,

207.play: They shall be praying nuns, not weeping queens;

208.play: And therefore level not to hit their lives.

209.play: KING RICHARD III: You have a daughter call'd Elizabeth,

210.play: Virtuous and fair, royal and gracious.

211.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: And must she die for this? O, let her live,

212.play: And I'll corrupt her manners, stain her beauty;

213.play: Slander myself as false to Edward's bed;

214.play: Throw over her the veil of infamy:

215.play: So she may live unscarr'd of bleeding slaughter,

216.play: I will confess she was not Edward's daughter.

217.play: KING RICHARD III: Wrong not her birth, she is of royal blood.

218.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: To save her life, I'll say she is not so.

219.play: KING RICHARD III: Her life is only safest in her birth.

220.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: And only in that safety died her brothers.

221.play: KING RICHARD III: Lo, at their births good stars were opposite.

222.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: No, to their lives bad friends were contrary.

223.play: KING RICHARD III: All unavoided is the doom of destiny.

224.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: True, when avoided grace makes destiny:

225.play: My babes were destined to a fairer death,

226.play: If grace had bless'd thee with a fairer life.

227.play: KING RICHARD III: You speak as if that I had slain my cousins.

228.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Cousins, indeed; and by their uncle cozen'd

229.play: Of comfort, kingdom, kindred, freedom, life.

230.play: Whose hand soever lanced their tender hearts,

231.play: Thy head, all indirectly, gave direction:

232.play: No doubt the murderous knife was dull and blunt

233.play: Till it was whetted on thy stone-hard heart,

234.play: To revel in the entrails of my lambs.

235.play: But that still use of grief makes wild grief tame,

236.play: My tongue should to thy ears not name my boys

237.play: Till that my nails were anchor'd in thine eyes;

238.play: And I, in such a desperate bay of death,

239.play: Like a poor bark, of sails and tackling reft,

240.play: Rush all to pieces on thy rocky bosom.

241.play: KING RICHARD III: Madam, so thrive I in my enterprise

242.play: And dangerous success of bloody wars,

243.play: As I intend more good to you and yours,

244.play: Than ever you or yours were by me wrong'd!

245.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: What good is cover'd with the face of heaven,

246.play: To be discover'd, that can do me good?

247.play: KING RICHARD III: The advancement of your children, gentle lady.

248.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Up to some scaffold, there to lose their heads?

249.play: KING RICHARD III: No, to the dignity and height of honour

250.play: The high imperial type of this earth's glory.

251.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Flatter my sorrows with report of it;

252.play: Tell me what state, what dignity, what honour,

253.play: Canst thou demise to any child of mine?

254.play: KING RICHARD III: Even all I have; yea, and myself and all,

255.play: Will I withal endow a child of thine;

256.play: So in the Lethe of thy angry soul

257.play: Thou drown the sad remembrance of those wrongs

258.play: Which thou supposest I have done to thee.

259.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Be brief, lest that be process of thy kindness

260.play: Last longer telling than thy kindness' date.

261.play: KING RICHARD III: Then know, that from my soul I love thy daughter.

262.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: My daughter's mother thinks it with her soul.

263.play: KING RICHARD III: What do you think?

264.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: That thou dost love my daughter from thy soul:

265.play: So from thy soul's love didst thou love her brothers;

266.play: And from my heart's love I do thank thee for it.

267.play: KING RICHARD III: Be not so hasty to confound my meaning:

268.play: I mean, that with my soul I love thy daughter,

269.play: And mean to make her queen of England.

270.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Say then, who dost thou mean shall be her king?

271.play: KING RICHARD III: Even he that makes her queen who should be else?

273.play: KING RICHARD III: I, even I: what think you of it, madam?

274.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: How canst thou woo her?

275.play: KING RICHARD III: That would I learn of you,

276.play: As one that are best acquainted with her humour.

277.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: And wilt thou learn of me?

278.play: KING RICHARD III: Madam, with all my heart.

279.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Send to her, by the man that slew her brothers,

280.play: A pair of bleeding-hearts; thereon engrave

281.play: Edward and York; then haply she will weep:

282.play: Therefore present to her--as sometime Margaret

283.play: Did to thy father, steep'd in Rutland's blood,--

284.play: A handkerchief; which, say to her, did drain

285.play: The purple sap from her sweet brother's body

288.play: Send her a story of thy noble acts;

289.play: Tell her thou madest away her uncle Clarence,

290.play: Her uncle Rivers; yea, and, for her sake,

291.play: Madest quick conveyance with her good aunt Anne.

292.play: KING RICHARD III: Come, come, you mock me; this is not the way

294.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: There is no other way

295.play: Unless thou couldst put on some other shape,

296.play: And not be Richard that hath done all this.

298.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Nay, then indeed she cannot choose but hate thee,

299.play: Having bought love with such a bloody spoil.

300.play: KING RICHARD III: Look, what is done cannot be now amended:

302.play: Which after hours give leisure to repent.

303.play: If I did take the kingdom from your sons,

304.play: To make amends, Ill give it to your daughter.

305.play: If I have kill'd the issue of your womb,

306.play: To quicken your increase, I will beget

307.play: Mine issue of your blood upon your daughter

308.play: A grandam's name is little less in love

309.play: Than is the doting title of a mother;

310.play: They are as children but one step below,

311.play: Even of your mettle, of your very blood;

312.play: Of an one pain, save for a night of groans

313.play: Endured of her, for whom you bid like sorrow.

314.play: Your children were vexation to your youth,

315.play: But mine shall be a comfort to your age.

316.play: The loss you have is but a son being king,

317.play: And by that loss your daughter is made queen.

318.play: I cannot make you what amends I would,

319.play: Therefore accept such kindness as I can.

320.play: Dorset your son, that with a fearful soul

321.play: Leads discontented steps in foreign soil,

322.play: This fair alliance quickly shall call home

323.play: To high promotions and great dignity:

324.play: The king, that calls your beauteous daughter wife.

325.play: Familiarly shall call thy Dorset brother;

326.play: Again shall you be mother to a king,

327.play: And all the ruins of distressful times

328.play: Repair'd with double riches of content.

329.play: What! we have many goodly days to see:

330.play: The liquid drops of tears that you have shed

331.play: Shall come again, transform'd to orient pearl,

333.play: Of ten times double gain of happiness.

334.play: Go, then my mother, to thy daughter go

335.play: Make bold her bashful years with your experience;

336.play: Prepare her ears to hear a wooer's tale

337.play: Put in her tender heart the aspiring flame

338.play: Of golden sovereignty; acquaint the princess

339.play: With the sweet silent hours of marriage joys

340.play: And when this arm of mine hath chastised

342.play: Bound with triumphant garlands will I come

343.play: And lead thy daughter to a conqueror's bed;

344.play: To whom I will retail my conquest won,

345.play: And she shall be sole victress, Caesar's Caesar.

347.play: Would be her lord? or shall I say, her uncle?

348.play: Or, he that slew her brothers and her uncles?

349.play: Under what title shall I woo for thee,

350.play: That God, the law, my honour and her love,

351.play: Can make seem pleasing to her tender years?

352.play: KING RICHARD III: Infer fair England's peace by this alliance.

353.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Which she shall purchase with still lasting war.

362.play: KING RICHARD III: So long as heaven and nature lengthens it.

363.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: So long as hell and Richard likes of it.

365.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But she, your subject, loathes such sovereignty.

366.play: KING RICHARD III: Be eloquent in my behalf to her.

367.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: An honest tale speeds best being plainly told.

368.play: KING RICHARD III: Then in plain terms tell her my loving tale.

369.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Plain and not honest is too harsh a style.

370.play: KING RICHARD III: Your reasons are too shallow and too quick.

371.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O no, my reasons are too deep and dead;

372.play: Too deep and dead, poor infants, in their grave.

373.play: KING RICHARD III: Harp not on that string, madam; that is past.

374.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Harp on it still shall I till heart-strings break.

375.play: KING RICHARD III: Now, by my George, my garter, and my crown,--

376.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Profaned, dishonour'd, and the third usurp'd.

378.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: By nothing; for this is no oath:

379.play: The George, profaned, hath lost his holy honour;

381.play: The crown, usurp'd, disgraced his kingly glory.

382.play: if something thou wilt swear to be believed,

383.play: Swear then by something that thou hast not wrong'd.

384.play: KING RICHARD III: Now, by the world--

385.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: 'Tis full of thy foul wrongs.

386.play: KING RICHARD III: My father's death--

387.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Thy life hath that dishonour'd.

388.play: KING RICHARD III: Then, by myself--

390.play: KING RICHARD III: Why then, by God--

391.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: God's wrong is most of all.

392.play: If thou hadst fear'd to break an oath by Him,

393.play: The unity the king thy brother made

394.play: Had not been broken, nor my brother slain:

395.play: If thou hadst fear'd to break an oath by Him,

396.play: The imperial metal, circling now thy brow,

397.play: Had graced the tender temples of my child,

398.play: And both the princes had been breathing here,

399.play: Which now, two tender playfellows to dust,

401.play: What canst thou swear by now?

402.play: KING RICHARD III: The time to come.

403.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: That thou hast wronged in the time o'erpast;

404.play: For I myself have many tears to wash

405.play: Hereafter time, for time past wrong'd by thee.

279.play: Let's whip these stragglers o'er the seas again;

280.play: Lash hence these overweening rags of France,

281.play: These famish'd beggars, weary of their lives;

282.play: Who, but for dreaming on this fond exploit,

283.play: For want of means, poor rats, had hang'd themselves:

284.play: If we be conquer'd, let men conquer us,

285.play: And not these bastard Bretons; whom our fathers

286.play: Have in their own land beaten, bobb'd, and thump'd,

287.play: And in record, left them the heirs of shame.

288.play: Shall these enjoy our lands?  lie with our wives?

291.play: Fight, gentlemen of England! fight, bold yoemen!

292.play: Draw, archers, draw your arrows to the head!

293.play: Spur your proud horses hard, and ride in blood;

294.play: Amaze the welkin with your broken staves!

295.play: What says Lord Stanley? will he bring his power?

296.play: Off with his son George's head!

297.play: NORFOLK: My lord, the enemy is past the marsh

298.play: After the battle let George Stanley die.

299.play: KING RICHARD III: A thousand hearts are great within my bosom:

300.play: Advance our standards, set upon our foes

301.play: Our ancient word of courage, fair Saint George,

302.play: Inspire us with the spleen of fiery dragons!

303.play: Upon them! victory sits on our helms.

1.play: Alarum: excursions. Enter NORFOLK and forces fighting; to him CATESBY

2.play: CATESBY: Rescue, my Lord of Norfolk, rescue, rescue!

3.play: The king enacts more wonders than a man,

4.play: Daring an opposite to every danger:

5.play: His horse is slain, and all on foot he fights,

6.play: Seeking for Richmond in the throat of death.

7.play: Rescue, fair lord, or else the day is lost!

8.play: KING RICHARD III: A horse! a horse! my kingdom for a horse!

9.play: CATESBY: Withdraw, my lord; I'll help you to a horse.

10.play: KING RICHARD III: Slave, I have set my life upon a cast,

11.play: And I will stand the hazard of the die:

12.play: I think there be six Richmonds in the field;

13.play: Five have I slain to-day instead of him.

14.play: A horse! a horse! my kingdom for a horse!

1.play: Alarum. Enter KING RICHARD III and RICHMOND; they  fight. KING RICHARD III is slain. Retreat and  flourish. Re-enter RICHMOND, DERBY bearing the crown, with divers other Lords

2.play: RICHMOND: God and your arms be praised, victorious friends,

3.play: The day is ours, the bloody dog is dead.

4.play: DERBY: Courageous Richmond, well hast thou acquit thee.

5.play: Lo, here, this long-usurped royalty

6.play: From the dead temples of this bloody wretch

7.play: Have I pluck'd off, to grace thy brows withal:

8.play: Wear it, enjoy it, and make much of it.

9.play: RICHMOND: Great God of heaven, say Amen to all!

10.play: But, tell me, is young George Stanley living?

11.play: DERBY: He is, my lord, and safe in Leicester town;

12.play: Whither, if it please you, we may now withdraw us.

13.play: RICHMOND: What men of name are slain on either side?

14.play: DERBY: John Duke of Norfolk, Walter Lord Ferrers,

15.play: Sir Robert Brakenbury, and Sir William Brandon.

16.play: RICHMOND: Inter their bodies as becomes their births:

17.play: Proclaim a pardon to the soldiers fled

18.play: That in submission will return to us:

19.play: And then, as we have ta'en the sacrament,

20.play: We will unite the white rose and the red:

21.play: Smile heaven upon this fair conjunction,

22.play: That long have frown'd upon their enmity!

23.play: What traitor hears me, and says not amen?

24.play: England hath long been mad, and scarr'd herself;

25.play: The brother blindly shed the brother's blood,

26.play: The father rashly slaughter'd his own son,

27.play: The son, compell'd, been butcher to the sire:

28.play: All this divided York and Lancaster,

30.play: O, now, let Richmond and Elizabeth,

31.play: The true succeeders of each royal house,

32.play: By God's fair ordinance conjoin together!

33.play: And let their heirs, God, if thy will be so.

34.play: Enrich the time to come with smooth-faced peace,

35.play: With smiling plenty and fair prosperous days!

36.play: Abate the edge of traitors, gracious Lord,

37.play: That would reduce these bloody days again,

38.play: And make poor England weep in streams of blood!

39.play: Let them not live to taste this land's increase

40.play: That would with treason wound this fair land's peace!

41.play: Now civil wounds are stopp'd, peace lives again:

42.play: That she may long live here, God say amen!
